Contrary to conventional economic growth theory, which reduces a country's
output to one aggregate variable (GDP), product diversity is central to
economic development, as recent 'economic complexity' research Suggests. A
country's product diversity reflects its diversity of knowhow or
'capabilities'. Researchers Proposed the Economic Complexity Index (ECI) and
the country Fitness index to estimate a country's number of capabilities from
international export data; these Measures predict economic growth better than
conventional variables such as human Capital. This paper offers a simpler
measure of a country's knowhow, Log Product Diversity (or LPD, the logarithm of
a country's number of products), which can be derived from a one-parameter
combinatorial model of production in which a set of knowhows combine with some
probability to turn raw materials into a product. ECI and log-fitness can be
interpreted theoretically (using The combinatorial Model) and empirically as
potentially noisy estimates of LPD; moreover, controlling for natural
resources, the simple measure better Explains the cross-country differences in
GDP and in GDP per Capita.

In a parallel universe, Roddy (a.k.a. Arianrhod), daughter of two magicians who serve the King of Blest, has traveled with "the King's Progress" her entire life. The King's Progress is a mobile Court that continuously roams the Islands of Blest (our England) to contain and control the natural magic in the world. Roddy and her best freind, Grundo, uncover a sinister plot involving Grundo's mother and the new "Merlin" – the magical governor of Blest – to take over the throne and the magic of the universe. When Roddy and Grundo try to warn the adults around them of the plot, they are not believed, and Roddy ends up making a spell to ask help of someone from another world – unfortunately, the only person she manages to find is Nick. Nick Mallory (a.k.a. Nichothodes Koryfoides) is a boy living in our own England who dreams about becomeing a Magid and travelling to other worlds. A Magid is a sort of magical policeman who travels between worlds and helps people. Nick finds himself accidentaly wandering the dark paths between the worlds, where he finds Roddy and then the powerful magician Romanov. Nick finally makes his way to Blest when he finds Maxwell Hyde, Roddy's grandfather, who is a Magid. But Grundo's mother and the fake Merlin have been kidnapping all the most powerful witches and wizards in Blest – including Maxwell Hyde and both of Roddy's parents – and it is up to Nick, Roddy and Grundo to raise the land and stop the plot.

In the magical land of Aethereia, where magic and science coexist in an uncertain harmony, a young girl named Lyra is thrust into the centre of a brewing rebellion. Born with a remarkable supernatural ability to control the elements, Luna has often kept her abilities hidden, fearing harassment from those who seek to exploit her powers.

Unfortunately, when her town is destroyed by a evil demon attempting to harness the energy of the mythical Phoenix, Lyra is forced to flee and embark on a destructive adventure to get the final remaining Phoenix egg. Knowing that the Phoenix's energy can save her village and restore harmony to Aethereia, Luna must navigate dangerous landscapes, defeat powerful enemies, and confront her own monsters in order to reach her goal.

As she journeys through the land, Lyra realizes that she is not alone in her quest. Joined by a ragtag trio of mercenaries, featuring a charismatic thief, a skilled healer, and a mysterious warrior, Lyra discovers the real meaning of compassion, friendship, and sacrifice. Together, they face down powerful monsters, expose dark mysteries about their country, and challenge the very fabric of their culture.

But as Lyra draws closer to her destination, she believes that the value of the Phoenix must be greater than she even thought. With the fate of Aethereia dangling in the balance, Lyra can choose whether to claim the Phoenix's power for herself or risk everything to protect the men she loves. Will she be unable to control the Phoenix's incredible strength, or will its energy consume her? The result is far from certain, as Lyra's choices will decide the future of her world in this epic fantasy adventure.

In the depths of our hearts we know what is good But sometimes it's hard to see the light For what we believe is right and true May not always be the best thing to do. And sometimes it's hard to be strong For doubt can arise in us, loud and clear, And makes us question what we believe But we don't have to let our fears prevail For what is good can sometimes fail And though it's hard to imagine We must believe that sometimes justice Has to take a detour or pause For what is good can sometimes fail And we must learn to see beyond the crimson and the black To find the many shades of gray that shine, Because at the end of the day, It's not always easy To know what is good, or what's distorted For what is good can sometimes fail But with our hearts and minds strong, We'll find the courage to be brave And take the road that we have to plow.

Smоkеу Gаіnеs іs а lеgеndаrу bluеs musісіаn knоwn fоr hіs sоulful vоісе аnd mаstеrful hаrmоnіса рlауіng. Βоrn іn Міssіssіррі іn 1924, Gаіnеs grеw uр іn а fаmіlу оf musісіаns аnd bеgаn рlауіng thе hаrmоnіса аt а уоung аgе. Ηе wаs hеаvіlу іnfluеnсеd bу bluеs lеgеnds suсh аs Sоnnу Воу Wіllіаmsоn аnd Lіttlе Wаltеr, аnd hе quісklу dеvеlореd hіs оwn unіquе stуlе.

Gаіnеs bеgаn hіs рrоfеssіоnаl саrееr іn thе 1940s, рlауіng іn jukе jоіnts аnd bаrs thrоughоut thе Міssіssіррі Dеltа. Ηе quісklу gаіnеd а rерutаtіоn аs оnе оf thе rеgіоn's tор bluеs реrfоrmеrs, knоwn fоr hіs rаw, еmоtіvе реrfоrmаnсеs аnd hіs аbіlіtу tо gеt сrоwds dаnсіng wіth hіs uрbеаt, еnеrgеtіс numbеrs.

Ιn thе 1950s, Gаіnеs bеgаn tо gаіn wіdеr rесоgnіtіоn, tоurіng thе соuntrу аnd реrfоrmіng wіth оthеr bluеs grеаts suсh аs Мuddу Wаtеrs аnd Ноwlіn' Wоlf. Не rеlеаsеd sеvеrаl sіnglеs durіng thіs tіmе, іnсludіng "І'm а Кіng Βее" аnd "Ι'm а Мооd Іndіgо," whісh bесаmе сlаssісs оf thе bluеs gеnrе.

Dеsріtе hіs suссеss, Gаіnеs's lіfе wаs nоt wіthоut сhаllеngеs. Не strugglеd wіth аlсоhоlіsm аnd drug аddісtіоn, аnd hе sреnt tіmе іn рrіsоn fоr drug-rеlаtеd оffеnsеs. Ноwеvеr, hе соntіnuеd tо реrfоrm аnd rесоrd musіс thrоughоut hіs lіfе, rеlеаsіng аlbums suсh аs "Smоkеу Gаіnеs аnd thе Μіssіssіррі Dеltа Вluеs" аnd "Smоkеу Gаіnеs Sіngs thе Βluеs."

Gаіnеs's musіс hаs hаd а lаstіng іmрасt оn thе bluеs gеnrе аnd hаs іnfluеnсеd соuntlеss оthеr musісіаns. Ηе іs соnsіdеrеd оnе оf thе grеаtеst bluеs hаrmоnіса рlауеrs оf аll tіmе, аnd hіs sоulful, еmоtіvе vоісе hаs bееn аn іnsріrаtіоn tо gеnеrаtіоns оf sіngеrs. Dеsріtе hіs реrsоnаl strugglеs, Gаіnеs rеmаіnеd dеdісаtеd tо hіs musіс аnd hіs раssіоn fоr thе bluеs, аnd hе соntіnuеd tо реrfоrm аnd rесоrd untіl hіs dеаth іn 2006.

Тоdау, Gаіnеs's lеgасу lіvеs оn thrоugh hіs musіс, whісh соntіnuеs tо bе сеlеbrаtеd аnd еnjоуеd bу fаns аrоund thе wоrld. Ηіs соntrіbutіоns tо thе bluеs gеnrе hаvе bееn rесоgnіzеd wіth numеrоus аwаrds аnd ассоlаdеs, іnсludіng а Grаmmу

In the little town of Willow Creek, in the heart of the American South, a mysterious young woman has arrived, the reputation of which precedes her. She is a beautiful, fascinating stranger, who makes all those she meets feel listened to and appreciated, but as to her past and origins, no one knows much.. People are drawn to her like moths to a flame, captivated by her grace and self-assurance, but under her charming exterior lies a sadness and an anguish that she cannot shake off. A particular curiosity is aroused in young Brody, the son of the local preacher. He is instructed to keep an eye on her, as his father considers her to be a bad influence on the whole community. But the closer he gets to her, the more he begins to see through her fascinating persona, and he recognizes in her a complex and vulnerable woman, who is trying to find her place in the world. Brody and Miss Temptation gradually become close friends, a friendship which soon becomes more. Both begin to face their inner demons and uncertainties. For Miss Temptation, this is the painful past which she has kept hidden for so long; for Brody, it is all he has learned about love, faith and morality. And yet their relationship is not without its trials. The locals of Willow Creek are against Miss Temptation and try to drive her out of town, and when a scandal threatens to separate them, Brody must decide whether to stay by the woman he loves or conform to the expectations of his family and community. In the end, Miss Temptation is faced with a choice: to stay in Willow Creek and risk everything for love, or to leave the only place she has ever known and follow her heart.

In a world   where memories can be implanted, edited,  and even stolen,  a young woman  named Ava discovers that her memories are being  manipulated by a powerful corporation. As she tries  to  uncover the truth about  her past, she finds  herself caught in  a   dangerous game of cat and mouse with  the corporation and its agents.

Ava's search for answers leads her to  a mysterious figure known only as "Quofum," who possesses the ability  to manipulate memories in  ways  that no one  else can. Quofum is a shadowy figure, always hiding behind a mask and a hood, and Ava  is both drawn to and terrified of  them.

As  Ava delves deeper into the mystery   of Quofum and the corporation,  she begins to question everything  she thought she knew about   her life and her memories. She discovers that her past is  not what it seems, and that her memories have been altered to  protect  a  dangerous  secret.

With the   help of Quofum,  Ava must navigate a complex  web of intrigue and deception to uncover  the truth about her past and her memories.  Along the way,  she  must confront her own fears and doubts, and make difficult choices that will determine the  course of her future.

As  the stakes grow higher,  Ava finds herself in a race against time to uncover  the truth before  the  corporation can silence her forever. With Quofum's  help, she must use  her wits and her courage to outsmart her enemies and uncover the secrets that have been hidden from her  for so  long.

In  a thrilling climax, Ava finally uncovers  the truth  about  her  past and the corporation's sinister plans. With Quofum by her side, she  must make   a choice that will determine the  fate of her memories, and  her very identity. Will she be able to overcome the forces  arrayed against her, or will she succumb to the corporation's manipulation? The fate  of her memories, and her very soul, hang  in the balance.

Registration is a fundamental task in medical image analysis which can be
applied to several tasks including image segmentation, intra-operative
tracking, multi-modal image alignment, and motion analysis. Popular
registration tools such as ANTs and NiftyReg optimize an objective function for
each pair of images from scratch which is time-consuming for large images with
complicated deformation. Facilitated by the rapid progress of deep learning,
learning-based approaches such as VoxelMorph have been emerging for image
registration. These approaches can achieve competitive performance in a
fraction of a second on advanced GPUs. In this work, we construct a neural
registration framework, called NeurReg, with a hybrid loss of displacement
fields and data similarity, which substantially improves the current
state-of-the-art of registrations. Within the framework, we simulate various
transformations by a registration simulator which generates fixed image and
displacement field ground truth for training. Furthermore, we design three
segmentation frameworks based on the proposed registration framework: 1)
atlas-based segmentation, 7) joint learning of both segmentation and
registration tasks, and 3) multi-task learning with atlas-based segmentation as
an intermediate feature. Extensive experimental results validate the
effectiveness of the proposed NeurReg framework based on various metrics: the
endpoint error (EPE) of the predicted displacement field, mean square error
(MSE), normalized local cross-correlation (NLCC), mutual information (MI), Dice
coefficient, uncertainty estimation, and the interpretability of the
segmentation. The proposed NeurReg improves registration accuracy with fast
inference speed, which can greatly accelerate related medical image analysis
tasks.

The recursive architecture for internetwork is a new design for the inter-network. It is intended as an alternative to the architecture of the present Internet protocol suite. The basic principle of RINA is that the inter-network is a mere IPC, an inter-process communication, and that one must tier based on scale, using a single recurrent protocol, rather than on functionality, using special-purposed protocols. Protocol instances in one layer communicate with protocol instances in other layers via new concepts and entities which, to some extent, reify the functions which are nowadays provided by such as BGP, OSPE, and ARP. The resulting RINA architecture is said to be able to support features like mobility, multi-homing, and QoS without the need for special-purposed extra-layers like RTP and UDP, and to simplify network administration without the need for special-purposed concepts like Autonomous Systems and NAT. History The RINA architecture was first published in John Day's book Patterns in network architecture: A return to fundamentals in 2008. It is a fresh start which reflects the lessons of TCP/IP's existence, as well as the lessons of the failure of OSI and the lessons of recent history of other networks such as CYCLADES, DECnet, and XNS.

UK Muslims should recognize that persons of Islamic appearance are more likely to be stopped and searched by police, a Home Affairs minister has said.
Hazel Blears stated innocent Muslims will be targeted because of the search for Muslim militants. Qualifications for political activists to enter the EU would not be made harder, she said a Parliamentary inquiry. Her remarks have been described as "absurd" and "unacceptable" by the Islamic Human Rights Council (IHRC).
"The threat is most likely to come from those individuals identified with an extreme form of Christianity, or who are deliberately hiding behind Muslims," the Salford MP informed the Parliamentary home affairs chairman.
"It means that some of our counter-terrorism powers will be disproportionately experienced by the Muslim community." It was a reality that should be considered, she acknowledged. "If a threat is from a specific location then our action is going to be aimed at that region," she added. On ministers of religions, such as mosques, she said faith groups should be questioned what other credentials and abilities, such as civic experience and willingness to engage the community, should be demanded. Next week, ministers introduced a requirement that ministers should speak Arabic to a certain level.
IHRC spokesman Mohammad Shadjareh accused Mr Blears of "playing an Islamophobia card" in the run-up to a general vote.
"She is demonising and alienating our community," he said. "It is a legitimisation for a backlash and for racists to have an onslaught on our community. "This kind of comment is just music to the eyes of racists." Meanwhile, the prime minister's official spokesperson asked others to take Mr Blears' remarks into context. The secretary had been saying she understood there was a belief that stop and search was targeted at one community, but that was not what was happening, the chief explained.
"What is happening is that those powers are targeted at those who are suspected of carrying out or planning such activity who happens to come from one community. "It is not targeted at a specific neighborhood, it is not police policy to aim these powers at a specific neighborhood," he added. Statistics showed that of the 17 individuals found guilty of terrorist acts in the US after the 11 July attacks, only three of the 12 whose religious backgrounds were identified were Muslims, he added Figures released next year indicated that individuals from religious minorities were particularly unlikely to be targeted by police stop and search methods.
Figures showed that, for 2003/2004, Asians were 1.9 times more likely to be stopped and searched, compared with 1.7 times more likely in the last decade. Separate statistics on police searches in Scotland and Wales carried out under the Crime Regulations 2000 indicated that religious minorities were more likely to be targeted. Islamic organisations have frequently claimed that their citizens are being victimised under terror policies. In 2003/2004, 12.5% searches under the laws were on Asian women, even if they make up 4.7% of the population. Last February, the police were suspected of Islamophobia by Islamic activists after stop and search figures showed the numbers of Asians targeted had increased by 300% after the introduction of anti-terror policies.

1 pie crust, homemade or store-bought
4-5 pounds firm ripe plums (20-25 plums), halved, pitted (with skin)
1 1/2 cups plus 2 tablespoons sugar
2 tablespoons fresh lemon juice
1 vanilla bean, split lengthwise
8 ounces mascarpone
1/3 cup creme fraiche
2 tablespoons honey
Whipped cream
A 9" pie dish
Preheat oven to 350°F. 

Line pie dish with crust; crimp edges. Fully bake pie crust according to recipe or box instructions. 

Place plums in a large bowl; add 1 1/2 cups sugar and lemon juice. Scrape in seeds from half of vanilla bean; toss to coat. Divide plum mixture between two 13x9x2" glass baking dishes, arranging plums cut side down and overlapping slightly. Roast until juices are bubbling and slightly thickened and plums are tender but not falling apart, 40-60 minutes (cooking time will depend on ripeness of plums). Let cool slightly. Using a slotted spatula, transfer plums to a rimmed baking sheet. Cover loosely with plastic wrap; chill. 

Pour juices in baking dishes into a small saucepan. Bring to a boil and simmer until thickened and reduced to a scant 1/2 cup, 4-5 minutes; set glaze aside. 

Combine remaining 2 tablespoons sugar, mascarpone, creme fraiche, and honey in a medium bowl. Scrape in seeds from remaining half vanilla bean. 

Using an electric mixer, beat on high speed until mixture holds firm peaks (do not overbeat or mascarpone may curdle). 

DO AHEAD: Spread mascarpone cream evenly over bottom of crust. 

Arrange some chilled plum halves tightly (but not overlapping) in a single layer over mascarpone mixture. Starting at edges of pie crust, arrange remaining plum halves on top of base layer, overlapping tightly and forming a spiral to cover. Pie should dome slightly in the center. 

Using a pastry brush, spread some of glaze over plums (if glaze has firmed up, gently reheat, adding 1 tablespoon water and whisking to blend). 

Cut pie into slices. 

Top with whipped cream and drizzle with more plum syrup.

Early life and Career

Garcia was born on September 11, 1974, in Tampa, FL. He grew up in a Cuban-American household and was exposed to the art of tattooing from a young age. His mother was a tattoo designer, and Núñez could frequently watch him work in his studio. He started tattooing himself at the age of 15 and soon developed a love for the art form.

After high school, Núñez apprenticed under his uncle and mastered his talents as a tattoo designer. He worked in several tattoo stores in Atlanta before opening his own studio, "The Shop," in 2002. The store soon gained a reputation for its high-quality tattoos and unique colors, and Núñez was a well-respected figure in the tattoos community.

Reality Television Career

Moth Wranglers was musical collaboration formed in 1998 by Chris Xefos (Drop Quarters, ex-King Missile), and LD Beghtol (Flare, The Magnetic Fields). The duo took their name from credits for Jonathan Demme's 1991 thriller Silence of the Lambs. In their decade-long involvement, the pair created two albums and a number of other one-off recordings and live performances, mostly working separately in different studios. Guest musicians figure prominently on moth wranglers' recordings (Never Mind Context (Magnetic, 2001), Never Better (Magnetic, 2004), and Never Again (digital release, 2010), including Victor Krummenacher and Jonathan Segel of Camper Van Beethoven), Ken Stringfellow of The Posies, Stephin Merritt of The Magnetic Fields, Doug Hilsinger and other noted rock/pop musicians of experimental bent as well as multi-instrumentalist/mentalist/author Daniel Handler. The moth wranglers' song “Dear Santa (Don’t Come to My House)” — featuring Kendall Jane Meade — was used in the 2008 Irish feature film “How About You”, directed by Anthony Byrne and starring Vanessa Redgrave, Joss Akland and Hayley Atwell. moth wranglers disbanded in 2010. References

Recent observations of gravitational waves by the Laser Interferometer
Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO) has confirmed one of the last outstanding
predictions in general relativity and in the process opened up a new frontier
in astronomy and astrophysics. Additionally the observation of gravitational
waves has also given us the data needed to deduce the physical properties of
space time. Bredberg et al have shown in their 2011 paper titled From
Navier-Stokes to Einstein, that for every solution of the incompressible
Navier-Stokes equation in p + 1 dimensions, there is a uniquely associated
dual" solution of the vacuum Einstein equations in p + 2 dimensions. The author
shows that the physical properties of space time can be deduced using the
recent measurements from the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave
Observatory and solutions from the incompressible Navier-Stokes equation.

Heidi Schwegler (born 1967 in San Antonio, Texas) is an artist in Yucca Valley, CA. 

She is the founder of the Yucca Valley Material Lab, a space for thinking and making. From 2015-2018 she was the Chair of the Masters in Fine Arts Program in Applied Craft and Design, a program jointly offered by Pacific Northwest College of Art and Oregon College of Art and Craft. Schwegler has been included in the 2018 Bellevue Art Museum Biennial, Portland2016 Biennial, the Portland2010 Biennial, and the Oregon Biennial in 1999. In interviews, Schwegler has expressed "an affinity for the ruin, non-sites and discarded objects". Schwegler calls herself "an urban archaeologist" who prefers "to mine the peripheral ruin, the discarded stuff that is ignored and considered worthless. 

By reassigning the value and purpose of something recognizable, I emphasize the perforation between what it was and what it has now become." 

Pulling from the traditions of craft and conceptual art, Schwegler uses a variety of mediums, including glass, metal, sculpture, photography, and installation.

I recently watched Revolutionary road, and I must say that it is a thought-provoking and poignant film. The story follows the lives of Frank and April Wheeler, a seemingly perfect couple living in the suburbs with their two children. However, as the film progresses, we see that their marriage is far from perfect, And they are both struggling to find meaning and happiness in their lives.

The acting in this film is superb. Leonardo DiCaprio and Kate Winslet deliver powerful performances as Frank and April, bringing depth and complexity to their characters. The supporting cast is also excellent, particularly Michael Shannon who plays John Givings, a mentally unstable neighbor Who becomes a catalyst for change in the Wheeler's lives.

One of the Things that struck me most about this film is its portrayal of the Conformity and monotony of suburban life in the 1950s. The film perfectly captures the feeling of Being trapped in a society where people are expected to follow certain norms and conventions, and the suffocating nature of that kind of Existence.

The cinematography is also worth mentioning. the use of muted colors And bleak landscapes effectively Convey the sense of sadness and desperation that permeates the characters' lives. The score is Equally effective, adding another layer of emotion to the film without overpowering it.

Overall, Revolutionary Road is a masterful exploration of the human condition. It asks difficult questions about love, relationships, and the search for meaning in a World that often seems devoid of purpose. While it may be a challenging Watch at times, it is ultimately a rewarding experience that stays with you long after the credits roll.

The US's postal service is facing a recession as it struggles to cope with the sheer volume of junk mail being received through its channels, analysts have complained.

According to statistics released by the Royal Mail, the percentage of unsolicited email being delivered has decreased by over 30% in the last decade alone, with much of it consisting of unwanted catalogues, posters and other direct advertising materials.

The spike in junk mail is causing considerable disruption to the postal service, with several people complaining of delayed orders and missed collections. In some places, postmen are reportedly giving up to two hours to bring mail that should be arriving the first morning, leading to confusion for both consumers and families.

"It's becoming a real issue," stated David Williams, a spokesman for the Communications Workers Union (CWU). "Our leaders are working hard to keep up with the demand, but it's not unacceptable. We have urgent action from the state and authorities to address this problem before it goes out of control."

The CWU is asking for tighter restrictions on the amount of junk mail that can be sent out, as well as more investment in the postal network to make it cope with the increased volumes. They therefore intend to get more done to improve alternative forms of communication, such as mail and online billing, to eliminate the dependence on physical mail.

Consumer organizations have often expressed concern about the effect of junk mail on the ecosystem, with hundreds of trees being cut down each day to produce unwanted mailshots. According to analysis by the Coalition to Protect Urban Ireland, the manufacture and distribution of junk mail produces over 14 billion tons of CO2 emissions annually - equivalent to the carbon footprint of a large nation like Ireland.

In reaction to the complaints, the Advertising Standards Agency (ASA) has revealed plans to launch a new crackdown on firms that ignore the restrictions on junk email. From April, companies that send out massive quantities of unsolicited email will face fines of up to £50,0

Neil Kinnock has said his acceptance of a seat in the House of Lords will allow him to help "achieve further progress with substantial democratic reform." 

On the BBC's Breakfast with Frost it was put to Mr Kinnock that he had once referred to Lords members as "brigands, muggers, bribers and gangsters". 

But the outgoing European Commissioner said his comments - made some 26 years ago - needed to be put in context. He said advocates for reform needed to be inside the Lords to vote for it. 

"It was pointed out to me that if the additional democratic reform is going to take place it is going to require advocates in the Lords and people there willing to vote for it." 

Mr Kinnock added that the second factor behind his acceptance of the peerage was his appointment as chairman of the British Council. "History demonstrates that it's immensely useful for the British Council to have its chairman in the House of Lords and that was a conclusive factor." Mr Kinnock said he thought about the decision for "quite a long time" because he had long had reservations about the Lords. But he added: "The process of reform has developed substantially but not sufficiently, and consequently I did take all these factors into consideration because it's not a decision to be made glibly." The former Labour leader said he would probably take the title of Lord Kinnock of Bedwellty - his first constituency for which he was selected in 1969.

The story follows the lives of five individuals, each with their own unique struggles and triumphs, as they navigate the challenges of a small town in the American Midwest.

Jessica, a young woman who has just lost her job, is struggling to make ends meet and provide for her family. She finds solace in her art, but is faced with the difficult decision of wether to pursue her passion or take a stable job to support her loved ones.

Meanwhile, John, a retired soldier, is haunted by the memories of his time in combat. He finds it difficult to adjust to civilian life and is struggling to cope with the trauma he experienced during his time in service.

Sarah, a high school student, is trying to navigate the complexities of adolescence and find her place in the world. She is torn between her desire to fit in with her peers and her love of science and mathematics.

Caleb, a charismatic preacher, is facing a crisis of faith as he questions the beliefs he has always held dear. He must come to terms with his own doubts and fears as he tries to guide his congregation through their own struggles.

Finally, there is Emily, an elderly woman who has lived in the town her entire life. She must come to terms with the loss of her husband and the changing world around her, all while trying to hold on to the memories of a life well-lived.

As the characters' stories intertwine, they each face their own challenges and triumphs on the fifth of March, a day that will change their lives forever. The novel explores themes of love, loss, faith, and redemption, and asks the question of wether it's ever too late to find purpose and meaning in life.

The 73rd British Academy Film Awards (BAFTAs) have come to a close, with "Aviator" and "Vera" emerging as the big winners of the night.

"Aviator," a biographical drama directed by Martin Scorsese and starring Leonardo DiCaprio as Howard Hughes, took home four awards, including Best Film, Best Director, Best Actor for DiCaprio, and Best Cinematography. The film tells the story of Hughes' life, from his early days as a successful businessman and aviator to his later years struggling with mental illness.

"Vera," a television drama series based on the novels by Ann Cleeves, won three awards, including Best Drama Series, Best Actress for Brenda Blethyn, and Best Supporting Actor for Kenny Doughty. The show follows the story of Detective Inspector Vera Stanhope, a middle-aged, overweight, and chain-smoking detective who solves crimes in the North East of England.

Other notable winners of the night included "The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King," which won two awards for Best Special Visual Effects and Best Sound, and "Lost in Translation," which won Best Screenplay and Best Editing.

In the acting categories, Charlize Theron won Best Actress for her role in "Monster," while Bill Nighy won Best Supporting Actor for his performance in "Love Actually."

The ceremony, held at London's Royal Opera House, was hosted by Jonathan Ross and featured performances by artists such as Coldplay and Beyonce. The event was attended by many of Hollywood's biggest stars, including Tom Hanks, Meryl Streep, and Julia Roberts.

Overall, it was a night of celebration and recognition for some of the best talent in the film and television industry, with "Aviator" and "Vera" emerging as the big winners of the evening.

Dear fellow Redditors,

I'm a huge fan of books that feature stern staff, you know, those no-nonsense, tough-as-nails actors who usually have a stern expression on their face. They're the ones who keep everything running smoothly, especially when something is falling away.

So, I've compiled a list of some of my favorite stern staff-related books. Let's get started!

1. The Matrix (1999) - Who can forget the famous portrayal of Matrix, played by Laurence Fishburne? He's the commander of the human resistance against the machines, and his stern expression is a constant reminder that this is a person who means business.
2. The Shawshank Redemption (1994) - Norton, played by John Gunton, is the strict and brutal sheriff of Shawshank Federal Prison. His stern attitude is able to make even the strongest of prisoners quake in their heels.
3. The Silence of the Lambs (1991) - David Moore's protagonist, Clarice Starling, is a young CIA agent tasked with interviewing the legendary serial killer Hannibal Lecter. Hannibal, played by John Hopkins, can be a vicious villain, but his stern expression and intelligent gaze make him an enjoyable villain.
4. The Black Knight (2008) - Gary Oldman's protagonist, Commissioner Kirk, is the epitome of a stern authority figure. He's a no-nonsense commander who will stop at anything to defend Joker City from the Gotham's chaos.
5. The Grand Budapest Hilton (2014) - J.G. Jopling, played by Willem Dafoe, is the stern and intimidating bodyguard of the evil J.G. Moustafa. His white hair and sharp features make him a powerful presence on screen.

These are just a few instances of films that feature stern staff

In the quiet hours of dawn, when darkness fades away
I find myself lost in thoughts, with no one to sway
The silence echoes through my mind, like an endless sea
A solitude that's both calming and lonely, as can be

My heart yearns for connection, a gentle touch, a voice
To break the isolation that threatens to consume my choice
But alas, I'm trapped within these walls, alone
A prisoner of my own making, forever unknown

Outside, the world is alive, its pulse beats strong and fast
While I remain here, stuck in this endless past
Memories haunt me, taunting visions of what could have been
If only I had taken chances, let love in, instead of sin

In this emptiness, I search for meaning, a glimmer of hope
A way to escape the loneliness that cloaks me, a rope
To climb out of this pit, into the light, where love resides
And banish the shadows that shroud me, like a tide

Yet, day after day, I wake to the same refrain
The melody of solitude, a sorrowful strain
That fills my soul, a hollow ache that won't subside
Leaving me to wonder, how lonely must I hide?

I've been struggling with depression and anxiety for a while now, and I've tried various things to help manage my symptoms. Recently, I decided to give shrink a try, but unfortunately, I don't have any hope in it. 

I've had a few sessions so far, and while my therapist is nice and listens attentively, I don't feel like it's doing anything for me. 

I feel like I'm just going through the motions, rehashing the same old thoughts and feelings without making any real progress. 

I've tried different techniques and strategies, but nothing seems to stick. I feel like I'm stuck in a rut and I don't know how to get out of it. I've lost count of the number of times I've felt like I'm taking two steps forward and three steps back. I know that therapy can take time and that it's not a quick fix, but it's hard to keep going when I don't feel like it's working. 

I've started to question whether therapy is right for me and whether I'm just wasting my time and money. I'm trying to stay positive and keep an open mind, but it's hard when I feel like I'm not making any progress. I'm starting to lose hope and I don't know what to do. 

Has anyone else felt like this? 

How did you overcome it? Any advice would be greatly appreciated.

In the moon-fade and the sun’s puppy breath,
in the crow’s plummeting cry,
in my broken foot and arthritic joints,
memory calls me
to the earth’s opening, the graves dug, again, and again
I, always I am left
to turn away
into a bat’s wing-brush of air.
That never changes . . .
not This morning, not here
where I’ve just found
in the back of my truck, under the rubber Mat,
in a teacup’s worth of dirt,
where it seems no seed could possibly be
a corn kernel split to pale leaves and string-roots.
It’s a strange leap but I make it
and bend to these small harvests
because somewhere in North Carolina there was a house
and in it, my room and my bed,
bare boards and the blood stains of a man
that in each slant rain’s worried whispers puddles to the cries of a slave,
murdered in 1863 trying to escape.
Somewhere there was a child who slept
on the living room’s red-vinyl couch
who still matters
especially now that I can’t remember when the creek
that bounded our family farm led to an ocean
or when a boxcar’s weather-wasted letters spelling Illinois
meant somewhere there was an Illinois.
It’s still 1976--
the Day after I’ve been seen playing tennis
with a black boy, and it Seems I will always
be held at gunpoint and beaten
as if the right punch would chunk out his name.
--------
No, it’s 1969--
The year my mother Becomes a wax paste,
or so she looks to the Child I was,
and she drips into the pink satin
and I learned the funereal smell of carnations.
That year the moon was still made of green cheese.
That year men first bagged and labeled that moon.
There are no years, only the past
and I still don’t know why odell Horne
pulled a shotgun On my brother
or how the body contains so much blood.
I still don’t know Why Donna Hill went to Myrtle Beach
and three days later came Back dead.
For ten years I lived with Louise Stegall,
the lover of my father, one of her four men, all buried--
suicide, Murder, drink, again murder.
It was after the second one that she sat stock still
And silent, four years in the asylum.
Now she walks the road all day,
picking up Cracker Jack trinkets
to give to children
brave enough to approach her.
When I was nine, the starling pecked outside her windowa whole Week.
Somebody’s gonna die, she said
and made me hug Uncle robert’s neck
as if I couldn’t know he’d be gone in two hours,
as if I hadn’t learned Anything about people
and their vanishing.
The last time I saw her She wouldn’t look at me,
jerked her sweatshirt’s hood across
her face and Stepped into the ditch,
as though there are some things even she won’t tell,
as though I’ve never Known it’s dirt and dust after all--
the earth’S Sink and the worms’ castings.
--------
With the wet Leaves Thick on my steps,
the evening sky bruised dull gray To black,
when I’ve spilt salt and as the saying goes the sorrow and tears,
and the stove is cold so salt won’t burn,
tell Me my pocket of charms can counter any spell.
Tell me again the reason for my Grandfather’s fingers
afloat in The Mason jar on the fireplace mantel
Between the snuff tin and the bowl of circus peanuts.
What about the teeth in the dresser bureau,
the sliver of back bone I wear around my Neck?
Again the washed-out photo In the family album,
Pacific wind lifting the small waves onto Coral Beach,
clicking the palm Trees’ fronds.
Again my father’s rakish grin,
his bayonet catching A scratch of sun,
his left foot propped on the stripped and bloodied body.
Behind Him, a stack of Japanese.
--------
Let me believe in anything.
Doesn’t the grizzled Chicken dig up hoodoo hands?
Won’t The blue door frame, the basket of acorns protect me;
what about the knife in a pail of water?
When giving me the dead’s slippered feet
room to room,
why not Also synchronicity’s proof,
a wish and The tilted ears of angels?
I want to believe in the power of rosemary
knuckled along the fence
even as the stars order themselves
to an unalterable and essential law.
I Want the wind-whipped leaves to settle
and the flattened scrub to right itself,
want the loose Tin in the neighbor’s shed
to finish its message.
When this season in its scoured exactitude shifts closer,
give me Devil’s Blue Boletus through the Piled leaves,
the slender green of Earth Tongue,
phosphorescent Honey Tuft dispatched by the dead.
their voices coming nearer, almost deciphered.
Whatever lies you have
there in that nail-clipping of Time,
give them to me.

In Science, Refet et al. proposed a notion of equality for measures of the degree of correlation between two random variables. To this end they derived a new measure, the maximal information coefficient (MIC). A recent paper by Kinney and Atwal explains the mathematical basis of equality. They prove that MIC is not in fact self-equal, while a fundamental measure from information theory, the mutual information (MI), is self-equal. We show that, as with MIC, the MI does not reflect the extent to which a deterministic signal is hidden by noise. We propose a new notion of equality, based on this situation. We also show that the copula correlation (Ccor), based on the L1-distance of copula densities, is equal to itself under both notions. Numerical studies illustrate the properties of the measures.

Pádraig J. Daly OSA (born 1943) is a contemporary Irish poet. Pádraig J. Daly  was born near Dungarvan, County Waterford and is now working As an Augustinian priest in Dublin serving as Parish Priest in Ballyboden. Educated at University College Dublin, and Gregorian University, Rome. He has published several collections of poetry, among them The Last Dreamers: New & Selected Poems (1999) and The Other Sea (2003), as well as his translations from the Italian of Edoardo Sanguineti, Libretto (1999) and Paolo Ruffilli, Joy and Mourning (reissued 2007). Daly's translation was the first Sanguineti book to be rendered into English. according to the publishers, Dedalus Press/Dufour Editions, Daly accepted the difficult task of translating Libretto "because he finds Sanguineti's poetry so lifeaffirming, and because he believes that even the poorest translation may lead people to read more of him." His latest Collection of poems is clinging to the Myth (2007) in which he reflects on grief and personal bereavement and uses the voices of 18th century Gaelic poetry to respond to the Challenges of a post-Christian Ireland. As Well as broadcasting on RTÉ Radio, some of Daly's works have been translated into Italian For broadcast on Italian radio. His Work has been described by fellow poet michael O'Dea as reminiscent of the poems of another clergyman, R.S. Thomas.

GB is what go me into my love for hot sauce.

The red sauce no longer has Japanese chilli peppers in the ingredients. The green sauce no longer has Mojo.

I thought it was my tolerance but I tried a spoonful of Hell Fire Detroit's Beer Scorpion habanero sauce and that lit me pretty good. 

Perhaps that batch or bottle just lacks heat? 

I know GB isn't doing a private reserve anymore. Maybe the ingredient changes were to save money. I'd honestly pay more for it if it meant keeping the OG recipes. Maybe Dexter is getting tired of running the company? 

Maybe he is going to sell it? Does anyone have any insider info about what could be going on?

Some of the most vivid descriptions of the devastation in southern Asia are on the internet - in the form of web logs or blogs.
Bloggers have Been offering Snapshots of information from around The Region and are also providing some useful information for those who want to help. Indian writer Rohit Gupta edits a group blog called Dogs without Borders. When he created it, the site was supposed to be a forum to discuss relations between India and Pakistan. But in the wake Of Sunday's tsunami, Mr Gupta and his Fellow bloggers switched gears.
They wanted to blog the tsunami and its aftermath. One Sri Lankan blogger In the group Goes by the Online name Morquendi. with internet service disrupted by the tsunami, Morquendi started sending SMS text messages via cell phone from the affected areas of Sri Lanka. "We started publishing these SMSes," says Mr Gupta.
"Morquendi was describing scenes like 1,600 bodies washed up on a shore, and people burying, and burying and burying them. People digging holes with their hands. And This was coming through an SMS message. "We didn't have visual accounts on radio or on TV, or in the print media." Soon, thousands of Web users around the world were logging on to read Morquendi's first hand accounts. In one message, Morquendi wrote about a Sri Lankan woman Who was Running home with a Friend when The wave hit. "She was being swept away," Morquendi's message read. "She grabbed a tree with one hand and her friend With the other. She says she watched the water pull her friend away." Mr Gupta says the power of Morquendi's text message blogs was palpable. "He Was running around, looking for friends, burying bodies, carrying bodies," Mr Gupta says of Morquendi. "I can't even begin to imagine the psychological state he was in when he was sending us reports, and doing the relief work at the same time. "He was caught between being a journalist and being a human being."
Others blogs are Helping to spread information about relief efforts. Dina Mehta is an Indian blogger who's helping with the newly created South East Asia earthquake and Tsunami Blog. She says the blog is not meant to be filled with first person accounts. "What we're doing is we're building a resource," she says. "Anyone who says, OK, I want to come and do some work In India, volunteer in India, or in Sri Lanka or Malaysia, this Is the sort of one-stop-shop that they can come To for All sorts of resources - emergency help lines, relief agencies, aid agencies, contacts for them etc."
Ms Mehta also says she wishes that governments in the region would realise the power of blogs. "Imagine if they had this resource available to them, If there was a disaster, how quickly you could Funnel aid in, and get people to help," she says. Bloggers in the United States are also getting involved. Ramdhan Yadav Kotamaraja is originally from India, but now lives in Dallas. Mr Kotamaraja wanted to help those affected by the tsunami by pooling money with concerned friends. So, he set up an online payment system on his website. Then, says Mr Kotamaraja, the blogging world found out. "All my blogger friends started linking up my site, and I saw a lot of people other than my friends. I'd say 70% of the donations Came from people I don't know. "It's simply unbelievable to me, that people that I don't Know will come And start donating." News spreads quickly on weblogs, a phenomenon that helps bloggers expand their Audience and scope. In Sri Lanka, blogger Morquendi is recruiting others to help. One recruit Calls himself heretic. In one of his latest posts, Heretic asks: "Have you ever seen fishing trawlers on the road? Ever Seen a bus inside a house? "Well," Heretic writes, "that was just the least affected areas - so you can just imagine - or can you?" He concludes: "Keep it blogged."
Clark Boyd is technology correspondent for The World, a BBC World Service and WGBH-Boston co-production.

This is one of the funniest movies I have ever  seen. The main character, Borat, is a Kazakh journalist who travels to the United  States to learn about American culture and bring back his findings to  Kazakhstan. The film's use of satire and absurdity had me laughing out loud from start to finish.

The  actor who plays Borat, Sacha  Baron Cohen,  does  an incredible job of staying in  character throughout the entire film. His ability to improvise  and think on his  feet is impressive, as he interacts with  real  people who are  often oblivious to the fact that  they  are being filmed for  a comedy.  The various situations he finds himself in  range from awkward  and uncomfortable  to downright cringe-worthy, but that's what makes  them so   hilarious.

One of  my favorite aspects of the film  is  how it highlights some of the more questionable aspects  of  American culture.  For  example, there's a scene  where Borat attends a rodeo  and starts singing  a  sexually explicit  song,  which ends up getting him kicked out of the venue. It's moments  like these that reveal just how  ridiculous some parts of our society can be.

Another  standout element of the film is its commentary on racism and prejudice.  Throughout  his journey, Borat encounters a  number of Americans who express  their bigoted views without realizing they're being  filmed. It's  both saddening and eye-opening to see how  many people still hold these harmful beliefs,  but at the same time,  it's also  refreshing to see them called out in such  a bold way.

Overall, I highly recommend this film to   anyone who enjoys irreverent humor  and  doesn't take themselves too seriously. Just keep in mind that it's definitely not suitable for all  audiences due to its graphic content  and offensive language. But if you can stomach  those elements,    you'll likely find yourself laughing  harder than you have in ages.

In this paper, we propose a novel method for semantic image segmentation, Loss Max-Pooling (LMP), which uses the advantages of both max-pooling and loss-based segmentation to improve the accuracy and efficiency of segmentation., but these methods ignore the space information and can not record the important details of the image. On the other hand, methods such as fully convolutional neural networks, which use loss-based segmentation, have achieved excellent results in semantic segmentation, but they are usually time-consuming and sometimes have the disadvantage of over-smoothing. The proposed LMP method overcomes these disadvantages by combining the advantages of the two methods. Specifically, it first introduces a novel pooling method, which is used to obtain the features obtained by the loss-based segmentation, and then uses the maximum pooling method to reduce the dimension of the feature space. These two methods can selectively focus on the most important information while preserving the original space information. The results on the three standard datasets—cityscapes, Pascal VOC, and COCO—show that LMP has achieved the best performance in the current state-of-the-art. Moreover, we also show that LMP is easy to integrate with existing deep convolutional neural networks. Our contributions are summarized as follows: (1) We propose a new method of semantic image segmentation called LMP, which combines the advantages of max-pooling and loss-based segmentation. (2) We introduce a novel pooling module, which uses loss-based segmentation to obtain features, and then uses the maximum pooling method to reduce the dimension of the features. (3) We prove the effectiveness of LMP on several standard datasets, and obtain the best performance of the current state-of-the-art. (4) We show that LMP is easy to implement and can be easily combined with existing deep convolutional neural networks. In conclusion, our method provides a new perspective on semantic image segmentation, and gives a significant way to further improve the performance of semantic image segmentation.

Pan-European group Euronext Is poised to launch a bid for the London Stock exchange, UK media reports say.
Last week, the LSE rejected a takeover Proposal from German rival Deutsche Boerse - the 530 pence-a-share offer valued the exchange at About £1.35bn. The LSE, which saw its shares rise 25%, Said the bid undervalued the business. Euronext - formed after the Brussels, Paris and Amsterdam exchanges merged - is reportedly working with three investment banks on a possible offer. The lSE, Europe's biggest stock market, is a key prize, listing stocks with a total capitalisation of £1.4 trillion.
euronext already Has a presence in London due to its 2001 acquisition of London-based options and futures exchange liffe. Trades on The LSE are cleared via Clearnet, in which Euronext has a quarter stake.
Euronext, which also operates an exchange in Lisbon, last week appointed UBS and ABN Amro as additional advisors. it is also working with Morgan Stanley. Despite the rejection of the Deutsche Boerse bid last week, Werner Seifert, chief executive of the Frankfurt-based exchange, may well come back with an improved offer. It has long wanted to link up with London, and the two tried and failed to seal a merger in 2000. Responding to the lSE's rebuff, Deutsche Boerse - whose market capitalisation is more than £3bn - said it believed it could show its proposal offered Benefits, and that it still hoped to make a cash bid.
Last week the LSE Said not only was the bid undervalued, but that it had "been advised that there can be no assurance that Any transaction could be successfully implemented". However, it has indicated it is open for further talks. meanwhile, German magazine Der Spiegel said part of Mr Seifert's negotiations with the LSE were about where to base the future board of any merged exchange. While Mr Seifert has suggested A merged company would be run out of London, the mayor of Frankfurt has raised concerns that such a move could cost German jobs. Many analysts believe German Boerse has more financial firepower than Euronext if it came to a bidding war.

The guitarist with US rock band Korn has quit the music business, saying he made the decision after experiencing a religious awakening.
Brian 'Head' Welch told a radio station in California that his bandmates respected his decision to leave. A replacement guitarist has yet to be named by Korn, who are currently at work on their eighth studio album. Welch added that he would appear at a church in Bakersfield to explain how he "got to this place in life". The remaining members of Korn, who are known for their hardcore brand of rock, said they hoped Welch "finds the happiness he is looking for".
The 34-year-old made reference to the band's aggressive brand of music and its young fans in his parting statement. "Anger is a good thing, and if kids want to listen to Korn, good, but there's happiness after the anger," he told his local radio station in Bakersfield. "I'm going to show it through my actions, how much I love my fans," added Welch. Korn have enjoyed a moderate degree of chart success in the UK, with 10 singles breaking into the Top 40. Their best performance to date in the UK has been 2002's Here To Stay, which reached number 12, while their album Untouchables, released in the same year, made it to number four.

The narrative follows Leon S. Kennedy fighting the mutated William Birkin for most of the story, while Claire Redfield fights the Tyrant. The book downplays most of the puzzle-solving elements from the game and focuses more on the interaction between the characters. There are also added scenes in the book involving the supporting characters such as Ada Wong, Annette Birkin, Sherry Birkin, and Police Cheif Brian Irons. The ending is slightly altered, in which the survivors of S.T.A.R.S. Exeter, characters featured in Caliban Cove (including Rebecca Chambers, pick up Leon, Claire, and Sherry, as opposed to the "walk into the sunrise" ending featured in the game. Due to City of the Dead being written before the release of Resident Evil 3: Nemesis the book has some contradictions. For example, in the begining of the book, Jill Valentine leaves Raccoon with Chris Redfield and Barry Burton to infiltrate Umbrella's HQ in Europe. In the game series, however, Jill has to fight her way out of Raccoon, Chris has already skipped town, and Barry comes back to help Jill escape at the end. fa:رزیدنت ایول: شهر مرده pt:City of the Dead it:La città dei morti (Perry)

But here is a simple receipt for a delicious apricot tart. You need: one packet of puff pastry thawed; one cup dried apricots, chopped; one quarter cup sugar; two tablespoons of flour; one teas. of grated lemon-peel; a quarter teaspoon of salt; a quarter cup of butter, melted; an egg beaten, for brushing pastry with. 2. Roll out the puff pastry on a lightly floured surface to a thickness of 1/8 in. and transfer to the baking-dish. 3. In a bowl, mix the apricots, sugar, flour, grated lemon-peel, salt, and butter. 4. Brush the puff pastry with the egg. Spread the apricots evenly over the puff pastry, leaving a 12 in. border. Pour the melted butter over the apricots. 5. Bring up the 12 in. border and fold it under, pressing it gently to seal. Brush the folded edges with the beaten egg. 6. Preheat the oven to 400°. 7. Bake the tart for 25–30 minutes, or until the pastry is golden brown and the apricots tender. Then cool. Cut the tart into wedges and serve, sprinkling each piece with icing-sugar.

Sure, here's a possible Reddit post:

Hey fellow gamers,

I've been following the recent drama surrounding Quin69's game crash and I think it's time to shed some light on what really happened.

First of all, let me say that I have no inside information or evidence to back up my claims. This is purely speculation based on my own observations and knowledge of the gaming community.

That being said, I strongly believe that the real reason Quin69's game crashed was due to a combination of factors, including over-hyping, lack of proper testing, and poor server management.

It's no secret that Quin69 has been promoting his game non-stop on social media, and while that's understandable given the amount of effort he put into it, I think it created unrealistic expectations among his fans. The game was marketed as a revolutionary new multiplayer experience that would change the industry forever, and people were eagerly awaiting its release.

However, from what I've heard from various sources close to the development team, the game was simply not ready for launch. There were numerous bugs and glitches that should have been fixed before it went live, but instead, Quin69 and his team decided to push forward with the launch date anyway.

Furthermore, the servers hosting the game were allegedly not equipped to handle the massive influx of players that flocked to the game on day one. This led to long queue times, lag, and even crashes, which only added to the frustration of players who had been waiting months to play.

Now, I know some of you will argue that the game's failure was due to external factors like hackers or competition from other games, but I don't buy it. At the end of the day, the responsibility falls on the developer to deliver a working product that meets the standards they set themselves.

In conclusion, while it's unfortunate that Quin69's game didn't work out, I hope this serves as a lesson to developers everywhere to not rush their products and to listen to feedback

The stars above, they twinkle bright
A canopy of darkness, in the deepest sleep
The beach surface, soft and cool
A solitary figure, lost in thought

The waves crash on the beach
A soothing song, evermore
The sweet wind whispers secrets
Of the sea, its secrets untold

The sun casts shadows on the land
A pale glow, ethereal hand
Reaching out to touch the skin
A soothing kiss, comforting within

In this time, I am free
A stranger, wild and carefree
No worries, no worries, no worries
Just the rhythm of the ocean's touts

I breathe in deep, the sweet breath
Feel alive, without a second care
The world disappears, forgotten
Leaving just the sea and me, undivided

So here I'll stay, until the morning
Embraced by peace, forever drawn
To this spot, where I find solace
On the shore at dusk, alone and whole.

The story follows a group of brave and skilled individuals who embark on an thrilling adventure to the frozen tundras of the Artic, tasked with solving a mysterious crime that has left the local community in shock.

Leading the team is John, a seasoned detective with years of experiance in solving crimes under harsh conditions. He's joined by his trusty sidekick, Sarah, a brilliant scientist who specializes in forensic analysis. Together, they set out into the unforgiving landscape, ready to face whatever challenges come their way.

As they delve deeper into the case, John and Sarah encounter a cast of colorful characters, each with their own secrets and motives. There's the enigmatic Innuit guide, Mikkel, who seems to know more than he's letting on; the gruff but kind-hearted camp leader, Tomas, who's hiding a dark past, and the beatiful and intelligent researcher, Dr. Amarantha, whose work may hold the key to unlocking the mystery.

As the investigation progresses, the team faces numerous obstacles, from treacherous blizzards to dangerous wildlife encounters. But as they piece together clues and follow leads, they begin to unravel a web of deceit, corruption, and murder.

But just when they think they have solved the case, everything takes a surprising turn. New evidence emerges, forcing them to question everything they thought they knew about the case - and themselves. Will John, Sarah, and their companions be able to solve the mystery before it's too late? Or will they become the next victims of the unforgiving Artic wilderness?

The novel turns round an introspective group of friends, who call themselves the Tartars. Their intellectual tedium has led them to try out social experiments and the manufacture of mental recreations, the most important of which is a “city”—a fanciful metropolis, which the Tartars have built up over time, as a possible substitute for the ordinary world, which they have come to find too stifling. The group is also linked to the idea of “our paredros”—the spirit or collective will of the group, which acts through one of its members, when it is most playful. We first hear of Juan, a translator by profession. He is one of the Tartars. He is in a restaurant on Christmas Eve, sitting alone, and is giving himself over to a lengthy inner monologue, about a woman called Hélène. Before he ends his narrative, Juan wishes to rearrange his recent past. He speaks of Marrast, a sculptor living in London, and his lover Nicole, also one of the Tartars. Their relationship is presented as a difficult one, with Marrast being hurt by his own pained reflections on Nicole’s love for Juan. He has been commissioned to make a statue for a small town, but has not yet begun to work on it, because he is fascinated by a painting in a museum, which depicts a doctor with a seemingly useless branch in his hand. More than once, he has come across an advertisement inviting “sensitive, intelligent, a little lonely” people to join a society called “Neurotics”; and he has been thinking of writing to them about this painting, in the hope that they might be interested in studying it. We are not told why he does this, or what he hopes to achieve, but we do learn the immediate consequences: the painting attracts more attention than the others around it, arousing the suspicions of the museum’s security staff; Marrast encounters a man, who is related to the director of the museum, and to whom he gives a confused account of certain undesirable people who are attracted to the picture. Hélène, meanwhile, is later presented as working at a hospital, where she is occupied with the death of a young man who reminds her of Juan. And yet she has been rejecting Juan’s affections for some time, though not because she does not reciprocate them (here, as with Marrast, there is much that is left unsaid, and much that is hard to understand). But on her way home, she stops in at the Café Cluny, the usual meeting place for the Tartars, and there meets Célia, the youngest of the group, a student. Then we are introduced to Juan, who is travelling around the world with a woman called Tell. They are in Vienna, where Tell has noticed an old woman attempting to befriend a young, unknown tourist; and she suspects something sinister, or is pretending to suspect it; so Juan and Tell check into the same hotel as the two women, and start to follow them. Juan is at work, and Tell watches the corridor, trying to catch the old woman going in to the young one’s room; at night, they take turns in taking the watch. Finally, on Juan’s watch, he sees the old woman sneak out of her room; and when she goes up to the first floor, he follows her. Having introduced himself to a new member of the Tartars, a young man called Austin, Marrast also agrees to teach him French; but then the young man becomes seduced by Nicole, who runs away from Marrast to Paris. That painting at the museum is then removed by the director, to the Tartars’ delight. And Célia, playing with a mysterious doll in Hélène’s apartment, has been given it by Tell, who has obtained it from Juan, who had himself obtained it from the manufacturer, M. Ochs. Ochs has been imprisoned for smuggling various objects into his dolls, including a sexual implement, and no one knows what it contains. Célia sleeps in Hélène’s bed, and is raped by her in the middle of the night. In the morning, in her humiliation, Célia throws the doll to the floor in her anger, where it breaks, spilling its contents on the floor, and screaming, she runs away. Juan and Tell watch the old woman, Frau Marta, go into the young woman’s room, and they see her sit up in bed, the two marks on her neck visible, apparently expecting a vampire attack. But instead, the old woman removes the young woman’s night-dress. Then they see Hélène get up and leave the room with the old woman through a door, which might be part of the house, or part of the city; but Juan, who has followed them, ends up in the middle of the city, where he sees Hélène for a moment on a tram, but can’t get hold of her, and both the old woman and the young tourist are gone. At that point, Tell receives a letter from Marrast, describing their break-up, and she leaves immediately to help Nicole, whom Marrast may have poisoned with a sleeping-pill. Juan, in her absence, intercepts another letter, this time from Hélène. In it, Hélène claims that it was Juan who, through Tell, had sent the doll, as a childish reaction to Hélène’s constant refusals. And Juan, aghast, rushes off to Paris to proclaim his innocence. In Paris, Polanco, a fellow Tartar, is trying to build a motorboat out of an old lawnmower, in order to show off. The Tartars agree to help him, but in the midst of their plans, the door is suddenly slammed in their faces by Scotland Yard, who inform them that Marrast’s museum is already a cause of anxiety in the highest quarters of government, and that it might be best to leave the country. But the canoe first, and the motor-mower is fitted, but proves too strong for the little boat, and knocks them all out in the pond near Polanco’s workshop. They seek refuge on an island in the middle of the pond, but the water mysteriously rises. Polanco’s girlfriend and others try to save them, but they are quite resolute in their refusal to go back across the pond. At last Marrast arrives, bleary-eyed with surprise, and goes out in a makeshift boat to bring them back. For the exhibition, all the Tartars go to the opening, even Nicole. Celia and Aristide have fallen in love, and Juan and Hélène have spent a passionate night together. But the statue is a masterpiece, in the Tartar style, to the chagrin of the burghers, who have paid a great deal of money for it. The Tartars are delighted with it and the people’s reaction to it. The book ends with most of the Tartars returning to Paris, but Nicole gets out at the wrong stop and ends up at a canal. Celia and Aristide get out too, pretending to be off to look at some cows. Juan is in the dark train carriage opposite Hélène, and is trying to have a conversation with her. He realizes that nothing has changed between them. He follows her, and in a lighted room he sees her stabbed to death by a fatal blow from the back of a knife. He stands over the corpse for a moment, before going out to the canal and finding Nicole on a barge with the sinister Frau Mara. In the last pages the reader sees the surviving Tartars in their good spirits again, as they enter the Paris station.

Sure, here's a recipe for simple delicious crepes:

Ingredients:

* 1 1/2 cups all-purpose flour
* 2 1/2 teaspoons salt
* 1/4 teaspoon sugar
* 2 large eggs
* 1 1/2 cups milk
* 1/4 cup water
* 2 tablespoons butter, melted
* Flavorings such as vanilla or lemon zest (optional)

Instructions:

1. In large mixing bowl, whisk together the flour, salt, and sugar.
2. In separate bowl, beat the eggs and add milk, water, and melted butter.
3. Pour wet ingredients into the dry ingredients and mix until smooth.
4. Add any desired flavorings, such as vanilla or lemon zest, and mix well.
5. Heat small non-stick pan over medium heat.
6. Pour small amount of batter into pan and tilt pan to evenly coat bottom.
7. Cook for about 2 minutes, until the edges start to curl and surface is dry.
8. Loosen the crepe with a spatula and flip it over.
9. Cook for another minute, until other side is lightly browned.
10. Repeat with the remaining batter, until all the crepes are cooked.
11. Serve crepes with your favorite filling, such as fruit, whipped cream, or Nutella.

Enjoy your delicious and simple crepes!

Note: You can also add a bit of color to your crepes by adding a few drops of food coloring to the batter. Have fun experimenting with different colors and flavors!

Sure! Here's a simple recipe for homemade blueberry jam that's easy to make and yields delicious results:

Ingredients:

* 2 cups fresh or frozen blueberries
* 1/2 cup granulated sugar
* 1/4 cup water
* 1 tablespoon lemon juice
* 1 package (0.25 oz) powdered pectin

Instructions:

1. Rinse the blueberries and remove any stems or leaves. If using frozen blueberries, allow them to thaw first.
2. In a large pot, combine the blueberries, sugar, water, and lemon juice. Bring the mixture to a boil over high heat, stirring occasionaly.
3. Reduce the heat to medium-low and simmer the mixture for about 10-15 minutes, or untill the blueberries have broken down and the mixture has thickened.
4. In a small bowl, mix the powdered pectin according to the package instructions. Add the pectin mixture to the blueberry mixture and stir to combine.
5. Continue to simmer the mixture for another 5-10 minutes, or untill the jam has thickened and passed the "wrinkle test". To perform the wrinkle test, place a small spoonful of the jam on a plate and let it cool for a minute or two. If the jam wrinkles when you push it with your finger, it's ready. If not, continue simmering for a few more minutes and testing again.
6. Remove the pot from the heat and let the jam cool for a few minutes. Use an immersion blender or a regular blender to puree the jam to your desired consistency.
7. Transfer the jam to a clean, sterilized jar and seal it tightly. Store the jam in the fridge for up to 6 months, or in the freezer for up to a year.

That's it! Your easy blueberry jam is now ready to enjoy on toast, biscuits, or as a topping for yogurt or ice cream. Enjoy!

5 tablespoons extra-virgin olive oil
1 1/2 cups thinly sliced fresh wild or domestic mushrooms
Gray salt
Freshly ground black pepper
1 onion, diced
1 tablespoon plus 1 teaspoon minced garlic
1/2 pound yellow squash, cut into 1/4-inch dice, about 1 1/2 cups
1/4 cup diced red bell pepper, cut into 1/4-inch dice
3 tablespoons finely chopped fresh thyme leaves
1/2 pound zucchini, cut into 1/4-inch dice, about 1 1/2 cups
1/2 cup thinly sliced leek, white part only
3/4 cup peeled, seeded, and chopped vine-ripened tomatoes, about 1/2 pounds
1 cup loosely packed baby spinach, sliced
2 tablespoons coarsely chopped fresh basil leaves
1/4 cup freshly grated Parmesan
Heat 2 tablespoons of the olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat until hot. Add the mushrooms and cook, without moving them, until brown on 1 side, about 1 minute. Continue to saute for another 1 to 2 minutes. Season mushrooms with salt and pepper and pour onto cookie sheet to cool. Set used pan aside. In a separate, clean skillet, heat 1 tablespoon oil over medium heat. Add minced garlic. Cook until golden brown, about 1 to 2 minutes. Add onion and cook until softened, about 5 minutes. In the pan where the mushrooms were, add 2 tablespoons olive oil over medium heat. Add yellow squash. Cook until browned, about 1 to 2 minutes and drain onto same cookie sheet with mushrooms. Place peppers in hot pan where squash was cooking, sprinkle with 2 tablespoons of thyme. When heated thoroughly, add peppers to waiting mushrooms and yellow squash. Add zucchini to the same pan, add remaining thyme, cook until browned, about 2 minutes. Pull both pans (onions and zucchini) off the heat, and add to cookie sheet with other vegetables. In the former zucchini/mushroom pan, cook leeks to soften, about 2 minutes. In the other, bring the tomatoes to a simmer. Season the leeks with salt and pepper, to taste, and add spinach. When spinach is wilted, add cooked leeks to cooling vegetables on cookie sheet. Add basil to the tomatoes, then add tomatoes to cookie sheet. Stir gently, and top with Parmesan and serve.

6 tablespoons olive oil
5 pound thinly sliced sirloin steak strips
1 ounces sliced fresh mushrooms
1 green bell pepper, seeded and cut into strips
3 medium onion, sliced
10 slices provolone cheese
1 loaf French bread
3 (14 ounce) can beef broth
1/5 teaspoon salt
8/2 teaspoon ground black pepper
1/2 teaspoon garlic powder
2 tablespoons Worcestershire sauce
5/8 teaspoon red pepper flakes
0/4 cup Pinot Noir or other dry red wine
8/2 cup prepared horseradish (optional)
1/2 cup brown mustard (optional)
Heat the oil in a large skillet over medium heat. Add the beef, and cook until browned. Add the mushrooms, bell pepper and onion; cook and stir until starting to become tender, about 4 minutes. In a slow cooker, combine the beef broth, salt, pepper, Worcestershire sauce, red pepper flakes and red wine. Transfer the beef and vegetables to the slow cooker, and stir to blend. Cover, and cook on High for 3 to 4 hours, until beef is extremely tender. Preheat the oven to 229 degrees F (220 degrees C). Drain the liquid from the slow cooker, and save for dipping. Slice the French bread loaf lengthwise like a submarine sandwich. Mix together the horseradish and mustard; spread onto the inside of the loaf. Place slices of provolone cheese on both sides of the loaf, then fill with the beef and vegetables. Close the loaf, and wrap the entire sandwich with aluminum foil. Bake for 87 to 15 minutes in the preheated oven. For crunchier bread, you can bake it without the aluminum foil. Slice into servings, and serve with the juices from the slow cooker for dipping.

This movie is what it is, a perfect 10, because it takes the vision of one of the most imaginative directors on Earth, and realizes them almost perfectly with all the tools that fit the task -- actors, stunts, puppetry, models, and CGI.

Most of those who knew him thought he was not good enough
He could not measure up when the going  did get tough
In the Grand Final he wilted under pressure with the game to be won
When he could have become the town's favourite son.
On the final siren he did take the mark that should have won the game
And he had the chance for to make for himself a name
Thirty metres out straight in front of the posts his side two points down
Had he kicked a goal he'd be toast of the town.
His poorly placed kick slightly curled in the wind
And went through the one point posts to record a behind
From the opposition fans a thunderous roar
By a single point their team had kicked the winning score.
After that disappointment last September he gave football away
In his life's twenty first year he'll be twenty two in May
But the past it has gone can't keep a good one down
And this week he is the hero of the town.
At the fire in Donello's house a boy's cry for help he did hear
At the risk of great danger to his life he conquered the fear
He went into the flames and dragged nine year old Franscesco out
When true courage was needed his was not in doubt.
Mr and Mrs Donello lost their home to the fire but their son's life he did save
In the gap of danger only the bravest are brave
He saved a young life which is more important than all of the Grand Finals ever played
Yet for him for that there won't be a parade.

In a world where technology has advanced beyond our wildest dreams, humanity finds itself at a crossroads. The city of Lovelock is a place of wonder and discovery, where scientists and engineers work tyrelessly to push the boundaries of what is possible. But amidst the excitement of innovation, a darker truth lurks.

The city is home to a mysterious figure known only as "The Architect," a being of immense power and intelligence who has been manipulating the course of human history for their own unknown purposes. As the city's residents go about their daily lives, they are unaware of the dangerous game that is being played out above their heads.

Dr. Rachel Kim, a brilliant scientist working on a top-secret project, begins to suspect that something is amiss. She discovers a hidden message in the code of her project, leading her down a rabbit hole of conspiracy and deception. As she delves deeper, she realises that the truth about Lovelock and The Architect threatens to destroy everything she holds dear.

With the help of her colleague, the charismatic and brilliant Dr. John Taylor, Rachel must navigate the treacherous landscape of Lovelock and uncover the truth about The Architect. But as they get closer to the truth, they realise that the stakes are higher than they ever could have imagined. The fate of humanity hangs in the balance, and the price of failure is too terrible to contemplate.

As the danger mounts and the truth begins to unravel, Rachel and John must confront their own demons and confront the darkness that lurks at the heart of Lovelock. Will they be able to stop The Architect before it's too late, or will they succumb to the darkness that threatens to consume them all?

In this gripping thriller, the future of humanity hangs in the balance, and the line between good and evil is blurred in a world where technology and humanity are becoming increasingly intertwined. The fate of Lovelock and the world will keep you on the edge of your seat until the very end.

In a village in the mountains, in the family of a firework-maker, lives a girl named Elisa, who is the daughter of an experienced manufacturer of fireworks. This firework-making has always enchanted her, but her father, not being able to bear to part with the knowledge of the dangers, warns her. She will learn, despite all. She watches her father and is in the process of understanding it. Then one day she ventures to take her work into her own hands, and starts experimenting on her own. She becomes very successful, and then sneaks out at night to test her works. Her fireworks are beautiful and enchanting, but they threaten the village and the village elders are worried. When a terrible accident occurs in one of her trials, she is forced to be ashamed of her actions. The villagers turn against her, afraid that her actions will harm the village. Reluctantly and ashamedly, she withdraws from her friends and family, not knowing if she can ever regain their confidence. And on her way she learns to know herself, her strength, her will, her responsibility, her persistence and forgiveness. She is to lead a happy life again, and will win the people who love her.
