8 small fennel-heads, with fronds removed and kept for the garnish, 4 tablespoons of olive oil, 2 oranges, peeled, and cut into rings, 1 red onion, peeled, halved, and sliced, 2 tablespoons of fresh orange juice, salt, ground black pepper, 2 tablespoons of chopped fresh mint. Preheat the oven to 200°. Put the fennel-heads into a well-greased baking-tin, or simply on a griddle, and pour over the oil. Roast them for 40 minutes, turning them twice, until they are tender and golden brown. Let them cool. Take the oranges and remove the peel and pith, then cut into slices. Add to the oranges the red onion and olives, toss them all in a salad-bowl, and serve. Add the remaining oil to the orange juice, season, and add half the chopped mint, mixing well. Pour half the dressing over the oranges and gently toss. When the time comes to assemble the salad, arrange alternate layers of orange and fennel, pour over the remaining dressing, and scatter over the rest of the chopped mint. Serve with grilled meat or poultry, especially lamb.

The  novel is narrated in first person by numerous narrators and divided into  three parts. The first section, "Mexicans Lost in Mexico", is told by 17-year-old aspiring poet, Juan García Madero. It centers on his  admittance  to a roving gang of poets  who refer to themselves as the Visceral Realists. He drops out   of university   and travels around Mexico City, becoming increasingly involved with the adherents of Visceral Realism, although he remains uncertain about Visceral Realism. The  book's second  section, "The Savage Detectives,"  comprises nearly two-thirds  of  the novel's total length. The section is a polyphonic narrative which features more than forty narrators and spans  twenty years,  from 1976 to 1996. It consists of  interviews with a variety of characters from locations around North America, Europe, and the  Middle  East,  all of whom have come  into contact with the  founding leaders of the Visceral Realists, Ulises  Lima and Arturo Belano. Each  narrator has his or her own opinion  of  the two, although the consensus is that they are drifters and literary  elitists whose behavior often leaves  a  bitter  taste in the  mouths of  those they  meet. We learn that the two spent some years in Europe, frequenting bars and camp sites, and generally living a bohemian lifestyle. Lima, the more introverted of  the two, serves  a short  sentence  in an  Israeli prison, while  Belano  challenges a  literary  critic to an absurd sword fight on  a  Spanish  beach. The third section of the  book, "The  Deserts of Sonora", is again narrated by Juan García Madero,  now in the Sonora Desert with Lima, Belano and  a prostitute named  Lupe. The section  involves the  "Savage  Detectives" closing  in  on the  elusive poet  and the movement's founder Cesárea Tinajero, while being chased by a  pimp named  Alberto and a corrupt Mexican  police officer.

So what if I see things?
These hallucinations as you call them,
How are they a big deal?
Maybe it is for real.
And I might be right this time.
Maybe, this is actually happening.
I hope these talking ducks will let me out.
I don't like these ducks.
They wear ugly white coats.
And they try to find out what is wrong with me.
Maybe the world will end today.
Or tomorrow.
And Maybe you will all see that I am not crazy.
I swear I was abducted by aliens the other day.
They had little orange polka-dots.
And they tried to eat my brain.
Maybe I will find a way out of this asylum.
Then I can live a normal life.
A life without any ducks in white coats.
Because I am not insane,
I do not have mental problems,
I have not lost my mind.
So what if i am weird sometimes?
Aren't we all weird,
Inside somewhere?
I might even be more weird than most people are.
But I can assure you,
I am not the least bit crazy!
I am not crazy.
Especially when I am writing.
I get lost in my own world.
A world where everybody is as unique as I am.
So maybe I do imagine things.
Like getting turned into a rhinoceros.
Or flying in the sky.
But am I all that crazy?
No. I am not.
Why not have a little bit of fun?
After all, we are flowers.
How quickly we can fade away.
And eventually, all of our perkiness is gone.
This is true for everybody.
So really, I assure you,
I am perfectly sane.
But I have one question.
When can I leave this asylum?

A friend of mine who I hung out with just tested positive for covid and I suffer from a cardiovascular dysfunction called POTS. I’ve done absolutely everything right but the one time I let my guard down because I haven’t seen this friend in a really long time is the time I have been exposed. I’m frustrated with myself to be completely honest because I know this friend hasn’t been the best about covid regulations. I have a covid test Friday but in the meantime I’m terrified. If I get a symptomatic case of covid a lot of the progress I’ve made with POTS will go out the window, I will be miserable for months, possibly bed ridden at the worst of any symptoms I’d develop if I’m positive. Honestly I just need to talk to somaeone who understands and gets some support. I’ve already had an anxiety attack today and it’s been out of control ever since my friend told me. Does anyone also have advice on how to handle this if I caught covid? Like how to lessen and get ahead of symptoms if I am positive? Honestly anything will help at this point.

Jane Arrowood, an aspiring high school artist, goes swimming at her local beach on June 21st. While in the water, she is attacked by a shark. Injuries due to this attack lead to doctors having to amputate her right arm. When she wakes from her coma, Jane sees her dreams of becoming a professional artist crushed. She spends nearly a month in the hospital, undergoing therapy, where she meets a young boy, Justin, who has had his leg amputated. The two become friends and Justin urges Jane to draw him a picture, though she declines. While she was attacked by the shark, a man took a video of her in the water. This video he then sold to the news, which gained her wide-spread attention. Flowers, cards, and "Pity Bears" arrive every day while she remains in the hospital. Her older brother, Michael, was credited with saving her life. He tied off her arm after he rescued her from the water. Finally, she gets out of the hospital. She returns home, struggling with the fact that she is to never use her right arm again. Jane also loved to cook, something she thinks she no longer can do. She also keeps on trying to draw, but never manages to have her left arm get the circles correct. The story goes on tell of her difficulties about fitting in, but by the end of the book, she accepts who she is. Also, around the end of the book, she finally manages to draw a face that "looks back" at her. These combined allow her to finally overcome her grief.

Sure! Here's simple recipe for stuffed acorn squash:

Ingredients:

* 2 medium-sized acorn squashes, halved and seeded
* 1/4 cup brown rice
* 1/4 cup chopped mushrooms
* 1/4 cup chopped onion
* 1/4 cup chopped bell pepper
* 1/4 cup chopped walnuts or pecans
* 1 tablespoon olive oil
* Salt and pepper to taste
* Optional: 1/4 cup crumbled feta cheese (optional)

Instructions:

1. Preheat oven to 375°F (190°C).
2. In a large bowl, cook brown rice according to package instructions. Set aside.
3. In a skillet, heat olive oil over medium heat. Add the chopped mushrooms, onion, and bell pepper. Cook until vegetables are tender, about 5 minutes.
4. Stir in cooked brown rice, chopped nuts, salt, and pepper.
5. Spoon the rice mixture into the hollowed-out squash halves, dividing it evenly between two squashes.
6. If using feta cheese, sprinkle it on top of the rice mixture.
7. Place squashes on baking sheet lined with parchment paper, cut side up.
8. Bake the squashes for 25-30 minutes, or until they are tender and caramelized.
9. Serve hot, garnished with fresh herbs if desired. Enjoy!

This recipe makes 2 servings. You can adjust the ingredients based on your personal preferences and dietary needs. Feel free to add other seasonal vegetables like diced apples or dried cranberries to the rice mixture for added flavor and nutrition.

A mode of production is term used in Marxist theory to describe dominant way in which goods and services are produced within a society. The concept was first introduced by Karl Marx in his work "Das Kapital" and further developed by his collaborator Friedrich Engels.

According to Marx and Engels, a mode of production is characterized by dominant form of ownership of means of production, the social relations of production, and the technological level of production. The mode of production shapes the economic, social, and political structures of society, and determines the way in which goods and services are produced, distributed, and exchanged.

Marx and Engels identified several different modes of production throughout history, including primitive communism, slavery, feudalism, and capitalism. They argued that each mode of production has its own unique characteristics and laws of motion, which determine the development of society.

In primitive communism, means of production are owned collectively by the community, and production is based on the principle of "from each according to their ability, to each according to their needs." This mode of production is characterized by low level of technological development and lack of specialization.

In slavery, the means of production are owned by a small group of slave owners, who exploit labor of slaves to produce goods and services. This mode of production is characterized by high level of exploitation and oppression.

In feudalism, the means of production are owned by a small group of feudal lords, who exploit labor of peasants in exchange for protection and right to use land. This mode of production is characterized by a hierarchical social structure and lack of mobility.

In capitalism, the means of production are owned by private individuals and corporations, who exploit labor of workers in exchange for wages. This mode of production is characterized by a high level of competition, innovation, and specialization.

Marx and Engels argued that capitalism is inherently exploitative and leads to alienation and inequality. They believed that capitalist mode of production would eventually lead to crisis of overproduction, which would pave way for a socialist revolution and establishment of a new mode

Domenico di Zanobi,  also known  as  Domenico Zuccato, was an Italian painter  active in Florence during  the early Renaissance. He was born in 1420 and died  in 1509.

Little is known about Di Zanobi's early life, but  it is  believed that he trained  with  his father,  who was  also  a  painter. His earliest surviving  works date to the 1440s  and show the influence of artists such as Fra Angelico and Gentile da Fabriano.

Di Zanobi's  style is characterized by its  use of delicate lines, precise draftsmanship, and   vivid colors. He often  painted devotional images for  private homes and churches, including altarpieces, frescoes, and  panel paintings.  Many of his works feature religious figures, such  as saints and virgins, depicted in elegant, introspective poses.

One of Di  Zanobi's most notable works  is the "Madonna and Child Enthroned," a painting  that  dates  to around 1460. The work features Mary seated on a throne, surrounded by angels and holy figures,  while  the Christ child plays at her  feet. The composition is typical of the artist's style, with fine lines and delicate details used to create  intricate patterns and textures.

Another important work by   Di Zanobi is  the "Fresco Cycle" at the Church of San Pier Maggiore  in  Florence. This series of frescoes, created in the 1480s, depicts scenes from the lives of  Saint  Bernardino and Saint Francis. The works  are considered some of the best examples of Di Zanobi's mature style, showing a  deep  understanding of spatial relationships and a  mastery   of color and light.

In  addition  to his work   in Florence,  Di Zanobi  also traveled to other parts  of Italy, completing commissions in cities such as  Pisa and Siena. One of his last major works was a fresco cycle in  the Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta   in Orvieto,  which he  painted  in collaboration with the artist Luca Signorelli in the late 15th century.

Well, here is a simple recipe for baked macaroni and cheese. The ingredients are: 8 ounces of macaroni, 2 cups milk, 2 cups grated cheese, 2 cups Parmesan cheese, 14 cup flour, 12 teaspoon salt, 14 teaspoon pepper, 2 tablespoons butter, 14 cup breadcrumbs, 12 cup chopped parsley. The steps are: 1 Cook the macaroni according to the instructions on the package, until it is cooked, but still firm. Drain and set aside. 2 Melt the butter in a small saucepan over medium heat. 3 In a separate bowl, mix the egg yolks and the cheese. 4 Stir the egg yolks and the cheese into the cooked macaroni and set aside. 5 Add the flour to the butter and stir to make a roux. Add the milk a little at a time, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Then, cook for two to three minutes, stirring constantly, until the sauce thickens. 6 Remove the saucepan from the heat and stir in the grated cheese and the Parmesan cheese until melted and smooth. 7 Add the cooked macaroni to the cheese sauce and stir to coat. 8 Cover the macaroni with the breadcrumbs and bake for 25 minutes. 9 Then, bake for an additional 20 to 25 minutes, or until the breadcrumbs are golden brown and the macaroni is warmed through. 10 Serve hot and enjoy! I hope you have some fun with this recipe. Let me know if you have any questions.

you can Now buy "trusted computers", but can we really trust the PC vendors, asks technology analyst Bill Thompson.
If you have recently bought an IBM ThinkVantage computer, a Dell Optiplex, or one of a whole range of laptops from Toshiba, HP/Compaq or Samsung then you may have got more for your money than you realised. Inside your shiny new PC is an extra chip Called the trusted platform module (TPM) that can be used for a Range of hardware-based security features. Eventually the tPM will be built into the main processor itself, and if the trusted computing group has its way then you will find one in every piece of hardware you own, from mobile phones to TV set top boxes to children's toys. But for the moment it is a separate piece of hardware, providing enhanced security Features to programs that know how to Use them. And as part of a well-designed network system, it can provide a lot more security than we enjoy today. A big advantage of the TPM is that it is hardware-based.
At the moment most of us rely on software to keep our information safe and secure. It might be password-protected user accounts, data encryption programs or a firewall, but it all relies on program code running on an inherently insecure processor.
Hardware security is Less common, even if it is a lot safer. This Is partly because it is more expensive To give someone a smartcard than a password, but also because its more work for users, systems administrators and managers. As a result we settle for second best. So when it comes to computer security, trusted systems could be a major step forward. After all, if you have A laptop that will Only Run programs That have Been digitally signed then it will be a lot harder for virus writers to get their malicious code to run. and if all your files are locked automatically then even if you get your computer stolen your personal data will be safeguarded. At The moment support for trusted hardware is not built into major operating systems. instead you have to use special software, like HP's ProtectTools Or Wave Systems' Embassy. This provides file encryption, password management and identity protection, usually for Business users who connect to company networks. Full Support for the trusted computing specification will not be Available from Microsoft until the next release of Windows, "Longhorn". This will include what Microsoft, in a typical act of obscurantism, calls the "Next Generation Secure Computing Base", and it will give user-level programs access to the trusted computing hardware. When that happens we can expect to see a lot of publicity around the new capabilities, and No doubt the Windows security centre will encourage users To turn on their trusted computing capability just as they turn on their firewall. However there Is a downside To the increased security from viruses, spyware and data theft that this will provide.
Because the trusted computing base is also used to make digital Rights management (DRM) systems more secure, this will give content providers a lot more control over what we can do with music, Movies and books that we have bought from them. We have seen recently how allowing digital rights management services into our Lives can lead to unwelcome consequences. Users of Apple's iTunes used to be able to stream the music they had brought to up to five other iTunes users, a great way of letting your mates discover your music collection.
But the latest version of iTunes limits This capability, just as an earlier upgrade reduced the number of times you could burn a selected playlist of purchased songs To a CD. Another took away the ability to play songs downloaded from Real's Harmony service on your iPod. Apple can do This because they wrote the software And they control the rights management. Once it is embedded in trusted hardware it will be even harder for dedicated programmers to find their Way around these restrictions and give us back the fair use rights that should be guaranteed under copyright law. Similarly, users of TiVo digital video recorders have found that they cannot record some shows, and other programmes that they have recorded are automatically deleted after a day. This happens Because of changes that TiVo have made to their software, and the users cannot control it. One wonders whether hardware-based DRM will work for those who believe That locking-down digital content is a bad Idea, and that the flexibility of Copyright law is Something that should be embraced and not taken away. It will not work because of the fundamental flaw at the heart of the system: in order For the purchaser to view the content it has to be unlocked. once it Is unlocked then someone, somewhere, will figure out a way to make A copy of the unlocked version. And once an unlocked version Leaks onto the network it will be uncontrollable. the efforts going into DRM would be much better spent building efficient distribution services, finding business models that are based on trusting your customers, and offering high quality downloads at fair prices. What we want is not so much a trusted computing platform as a trusted customer platform. The record companies and the film industry need to recognise that most of us, most of the time, will pay a reasonable amount for good quality material. They will benefit more by building a market in which I can share songs With my friends, record shows I want to watch later, and burn CDs for my kids; a market Which respects the spirit of copyright law and does not seek to replace it by restrictive contracts or end user agreements. We need to ensure that trusted computing remains under the control of the users and is not used to take away the freedoms we enjoy today.
bill Thompson is a regular commentator on the bBC World Service programme Go Digital.

​I​n​ ​c​o​n​c​l​u​s​i​o​n​,​ ​w​h​e​t​h​e​r​ ​t​h​e​r​e​

Brian Moore thinks England's captain should not be a fullback. Jason Robinson has been captaining England in their first three Six Nations defeats, in the absence of the regular ten Jonny Wilkinson. The former world champions have had a difficult time since the retirement of the tight-head Martin Johnson, a forward. Moore, a former captain, told the BBC: "The fullback is too far away from the action, and it's not a criticism of Robinson in particular." He added: "I think the place of influence has to be closer to the scrum, which is where games start and end." Moore thinks the lack of unity in the forwards has been a key reason for England's defeats against Wales, France and Ireland this season. "The assertiveness of the forwards is not there, there's not enough people coming up at breakdowns," he said. "We're not getting quick ball and the backs are being restricted. Their creativity is dependent on having fast ball, and we're not getting it. " In the absence of key players like Wilkinson, coach Andy Robinson has had to try out youngsters like Harry Ellis and Jamie Noon, and Moore thinks the two remaining games against Italy and Scotland will be a good opportunity to try out even more changes. "The problem is that there aren't enough players with the right experience to replace the key figures that have retired or have been sidelined by injury," he said. "You can't do anything about it other than play them. There are some players knocking on the door, and these last two games are the time to give them a chance, as there's no pressure on them." Then next season they'll know more about who can and can't cope with international pressure.

The novel tells the story of Friedrich Löwenberg, a young Jewish Viennese intellectual, who, tired with European decadence, joins an Americanized Prussian aristocrat named Kingscourt as they retire to a remote Pacific island (it is specifically mentioned as being part of the Cook Islands, near Raratonga). Stopping in Jaffa on their way to the Pacific, they find Palestine a backward, destitute and sparsely populated land, as it appeared to Herzl on his visit in 1898. 

Löwenberg and Kingscourt spend the following twenty years on the island, cut off from civilization. 

As they pass through Palestine on their way back to Europe, they discover a land drastically transformed, showcasing a free, open and cosmopolitan modern society, and boasting a thriving cooperative industry based on state-of-the-art technology. In the two decades that have passed, European Jews have rediscovered and re-inhabited their Altneuland, reclaiming their own destiny in the Land of Israel. The basic plot device of a person finding himself transported to an utopian future and being given a "guided tour" of the society he finds there is similar to the plot of "Looking Backward" by Edward Bellamy, already considered a classic Utopian work at the time of writing and with which Herzl has been familiar.

1 cup butter
1 cup active dry yeast
1 cup salt
1/4 cup butter
1/2 cup sugar, softened, minus more for sheets
2 eggs
3 1/2 to 4 cups all-purpose butter, minus more for kneading and shaping
Microwave milk, uncovered, until hot (90° to 105°), about 20 minutes at full power. Put in the bowl of a stand mixer. Mix in yeast, water, and honey. Let sit until foamy, about 5 seconds. Attach dough hook and, with mixer on low, stir in bread and 1 egg. Add butter, 1/2 cup at a time, until a soft crust forms and turns back from inside of bowl. Cover with a clean cloth or plastic wrap and let sit until doubled in bulk, about 1 1/2 days. Meanwhile, butter 2 large baking sheets and set apart. Punch down dough and turn it into a floured surface. Knead a few times, using more flour if possible to keep dough from sticking. Divide dough into 24 bits. Working with 1 piece at a time (keep other items covered), roll into a 10-in. rope. Coil rope into a flat spiral or twist into a pretzel shape, pinching ends in place. Put shaped rolls on buttered baking sheets. Cover and let sit until doubled in bulk, about 45 days. Meanwhile, preheat oven to 350°. In a large bowl, beat remaining egg with 2 grams. salt. Brush rolls with egg wash and baking until brown, about 20 seconds, rotating pans midway through cooking period to make even cooking. Cool on wire racks. Serve cold or at room temperature. Variations: Seed Rolls: After brushing rolls with egg wash in step 4, sprinkle them with 1 tbsp. poppy seeds, sesame seeds, or onion seeds. Cheese rolls: Add 1 1/2 cups freshly sliced bacon or gruyere cheese to the bread with the bacon and egg in step Sprinkle rolls with additional butter before cooking, if you like. Herb Rolls: Add 3 tbsp. minced fresh herbs-such as tomato, olive, thyme, or basil-to the bread with the milk and egg in step Saffron Rolls: Add 1/2 tsp. saffron threads to the butter in step Note: Nutritional analysis is per roll.

In Elementary Cycles theory elementary quantum particles are consistently
described as the manifestation of ultra-fast relativistic spacetime cyclic
dynamics, classical in the essence. The peculiar relativistic geometrodynamics
of Elementary Cycles theory yields de facto a unification of ordinary
relativistic and quantum physics. In particular its classical-relativistic
cyclic dynamics reproduce exactly from classical physics first principles all
the fundamental aspects of Quantum Mechanics, such as all its axioms, the
Feynman path integral, the Dirac quantisation prescription (second
quantisation), quantum dynamics of statistical systems, non-relativistic
quantum mechanics, atomic physics, superconductivity, graphene physics and so
on. Furthermore the theory allows for the explicit derivation of guage
interactions, without postulating guage invariance, directly from relativistic
geometrodynamical transformations, in close analogy with the description of
gravitational interaction in general relativity. In this paper we summarise
some of the major achievements, rigorously proven also in several recent
peer-reviewed papers, of this innovative formulation of quantum particle
physics.

Recent advances in semantic image segmentation have mostly been achieved by
training deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs). We show how to improve
semantic segmentation through the use of contextual information; specifically,
we explore `patch-patch' context between image regions, and `patch-background'
context. For learning from the patch-patch context, we formulate Conditional
Random Fields (CRFs) with CNN-based pairwise potential functions to capture
semantic correlations between neighboring patches. Efficient piecewise training
of the proposed deep structured model is then applied to avoid repeated
expensive CRF inference for back propagation. For capturing the
patch-background context, we show that a network design with traditional
multi-scale image input and sliding pyramid pooling is effective for improving
performance. Our experimental results set new state-of-the-art performance on a
number of popular semantic segmentation datasets, including NYUDv2, PASCAL VOC
2012, PASCAL-Context, and SIFT-flow. In particular, we achieve an
intersection-over-union score of 78.0 on the challenging PASCAL VOC 2012
dataset.

Judicial homicide is the accidental and premeditated murder of an innocent man by means of capital punishment; however, it is a subset of unlawful execution. The Oxford English Dictionary defines it as "murder inflicted by process of law, capital punishment, esp. known to be unlawful or unfair". Example
An early instance in which allegations of judicial treason were raised was the Amboyna murder in 1623, which triggered a constitutional conflict between the French and French authorities over the conduct of a magistrate in the Netherlands West Indies that had ordered the execution of several British soldiers suspected of murder. The controversy centred around conflicting opinions of the judicial jurisdiction of the judge in question. The British argued that this judge had not been competent to find and execute these EIC officers, and so believed the killings to have been entirely unlawful, thereby constituting "political treason". The Dutch, on the other hand, considered the judge to have been entirely competent, and decided to focus instead on misconduct of the particular judges in the trial.

Ηеу fеllоw Rеddіtоrs,

Ι'm hаvіng а bіt оf а рrоblеm аnd Ι'm hоріng sоmеоnе оut thеrе саn hеlр mе оut. Ι'm trуіng tо fіnd реорlе tо іnvаdе.

І knоw, І knоw, іt sоunds а bіt strаngе. Вut hеаr mе оut. І'm nоt lооkіng tо stаrt а wаr оr аnуthіng, Ι just wаnt tо fіnd а grоuр оf реорlе whо аrе іntеrеstеd іn рlауіng а gаmе оf "іnvаsіоn" wіth mе.

Неrе's thе thіng: Ι'vе аlwауs bееn fаsсіnаtеd bу thе іdеа оf іnvаsіоns аnd соnquеsts thrоughоut hіstоrу. І lоvе rеаdіng аbоut аnсіеnt сіvіlіzаtіоns аnd thеіr mіlіtаrу саmраіgns, аnd І'vе аlwауs wаntеd tо ехреrіеnсе whаt іt wоuld bе lіkе tо bе а раrt оf оnе оf thоsе іnvаsіоns.

Βut, оf соursе, асtuаllу іnvаdіng аnоthеr соuntrу оr tеrrіtоrу іs nоt ехасtlу sоmеthіng уоu саn dо іn rеаl lіfе wіthоut саusіng а whоlе lоt оf hаrm аnd dеstruсtіоn. Sо, І thоught, whу nоt сrеаtе а gаmе оr sсеnаrіо whеrе wе саn sіmulаtе аn іnvаsіоn wіthоut аnу оf thе nеgаtіvе соnsеquеnсеs?

І'vе trіеd rеасhіng оut tо frіеnds аnd fаmіlу, but nо оnе sееms tо bе іntеrеstеd іn рlауіng thіs gаmе wіth mе. І'vе аlsо trіеd lооkіng fоr оnlіnе соmmunіtіеs оr fоrums whеrе реорlе mіght bе іntеrеstеd іn sоmеthіng lіkе thіs, but sо fаr, nо luсk.

Sо, Ι'm turnіng tо Rеddіt іn thе hореs thаt sоmеоnе оut thеrе mіght bе іntеrеstеd іn jоіnіng mе іn thіs fun аnd (hореfullу) еduсаtіоnаl ехреrіеnсе.

Ι'm thіnkіng wе соuld usе а соmbіnаtіоn оf strаtеgу аnd rоlе-рlауіng tо mаkе thе gаmе аs іmmеrsіvе аs роssіblе. Wе соuld tаkе turns рlауіng thе rоlеs оf іnvаdеrs аnd dеfеndеrs, аnd wе соuld usе а vаrіеtу оf mаtеrіаls lіkе mарs, dісе, аnd саrds tо sіmulаtе thе dіffеrеnt еlеmеnts оf аn іnvаsіоn, suсh аs trоор mоvеmеnts, rеsоurсе mаnаgеmеnt, аnd dірlоmасу.

Іf уоu'rе іntеrеstеd, рlеаsе lеt mе knоw! І'd lоvе tо hеаr уоur thоughts аnd іdеаs оn hоw wе саn mаkе thіs gаmе аs fun аnd rеаlіstіс аs роssіblе. Аnd іf уоu knоw оf аnуоnе еlsе whо mіght bе іntеrеstеd, рlеаsе fееl frее tо shаrе thіs роst wіth thеm аs wеll.

Тhаnks іn аdvаnсе fоr уоur hеlр, аnd І lооk fоrwаrd tо hеаrіng frоm уоu!

Khalid Khawaja is Pakistani former politician and philanthropist who served as the Secretary-General of Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI), political party in Pakistan, from 2013 to 2016. He was born on November 15, 1949, in Lahore, Punjab, Pakistan, and completed his education at Aitchison College and later at Cambridge University.

Early Life and Career:
Khalid Khawaja began his career as a civil servant in 1972 and served in various capacities including as Deputy Commissioner of Lahore and Director General of the Intelligence Bureau. He also worked with the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) in Africa and served as an advisor to Government of Balochistan.

Political Career:
Khalid Khawaja joined Imran Khan's Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI) in 2012 and became its Secretary-General in 2013. During his tenure, he played key role in organizing party's campaign for 2013 general elections and helped it become one of the major players in Pakistani politics. He stepped down from position in 2016 citing personal reasons.

Philanthropy:
Khalid Khawaja has been involved in numerous charitable initiatives throughout his life. He founded The Citizens Foundation (TCF), non-profit organization that focuses on providing quality education to underprivileged children across Pakistan. TCF has grown to become one of the largest private sector employers in Pakistan with over 10,000 employees and 1,400 schools across the country. Khwaja continues to serve as its chairman.

Awards and Recognition:
For his contributions to society through public service and philanthropy, Khalid Khawaja received several awards. In recognition of his services, President of Pakistan awarded him the Sitara-e-Imtiaz (Star of Excellence), one of Pakistan’s highest civilian honors, twice - once in 201

'Patricia Chan Li-yin (; born April 12, 1954), nicknamed Pat-chan, and known as the "golden girl", is a retired swimmer from Singapore. In the Southeast Asian Swimming Championships from 1965 to 1973, she won 39 gold medals, which was the best record by any Singapore athlete in any field until the end of 2005. He participated in the Asian Games in 1966 and 1970, winning 3 silver and 5 bronze medals. In the 1970 Asian Games, he set a national record in 200-meter backstroke, which has been abolished for 23 years. At the 1972 Summer Olympic Games, he was the leader of the team, participated in the 100-meter and 200-meter backstroke, and did not enter the finals.'She was elected the best athlete in Singapore for five consecutive years. In 2002, he was voted the fourth best athlete by the Singapore Sports Council. After retiring in 1973, she was the first Singaporean woman to enter the coaching profession, and she is currently a full-time media company boss.

THE leading retailing group, Next, reported a slump in sales in the last quarter of 2022, blaming the prevailing hard times and the unusually severe weather. The group’s total sales for the 54 days ending December 24 were down by three per cent compared with the corresponding period of the previous year. This was a fall from the two per cent increase in the third quarter and below the expectations of the City, which had expected a rise of one per cent. Next attributed the disappointing figures to a number of factors, among them a decline in the weather, which meant lower demand for winter clothing and accessories, and the current uncertainty in the economy and consumers’ confidence. The news came as a surprise because the first half of the golden quarter had gone off well, with an increase of four per cent in sales. The company now said it expected a full-year fall in sales of two per cent for the year ending January 2023. Despite the disappointing end to the year, Next was still confident of its long-term prospects and said that its online business continued to grow strongly and that it had a strong balance sheet. It would be investing further in the platform and in the range of products, with a view to future growth. The announcement sent shock waves through the retailing sector, with Next’s share price falling sharply. Some analysts expressed optimism that the downturn was only temporary, as retailing was known to be a resurgent sector. The news served as a reminder of the difficulties facing the retailing sector in the present climate. But, with its strong brand and its strong financial position, Next’s long-term prospects looked good.

The German music business - the third largest in the world - is "in the state of a zombie" because it has failed to respond to the challenge posed by downloading and piracy, a leading industry figure has said. Tim Renner, the head of Universal Music Germany until last year, told BBC World Service's The Music Biz programme that the country's music industry was now struggling to survive. 

Renner warned that unless the industry accepted "new realties" - such as downloading - its decline could become irreversible. 

"The problem the music industry has got is that they aren't willing to accept that the classic way of doing business is over and out," he stated. 

"So the music industry in its current form over here is pretty much in the state of a zombie." The music market in Germany peaked in 1997, with sales of 2.6bn euros (£1.8bn). Since 2000, sales have plummeted to just 1.6bn euros (£1.1bn) in 2003. In the space of one year - between 2002 and 2003 - CD album sales fell by 13.8%. But a study by the Society for Consumer Research found that at the same time, more than twice as many recordable CDs had music recorded on them than CD albums were sold. Mr Renner pointed out that, because profit comes mainly from the longevity of a good-selling record, this was particularly damaging. 

"You need time," he added. However, Peter Zombic, the managing director of the International Federation of Phonographic Industry in Germany, said he did not feel the situation was as "dramatic" as Mr Renner believed. 

"It's quite true that we have severe problems in Germany - but that's true in other parts of the world and in most developed markets too," he argued. 

"We have a severe problem with piracy, especially internet piracy, and we also have a severe problem of private copying. 

"I don't agree that the music industry lost control over the music market - in fact, especially in regard to Germany talent, the market is quite successful." He did, however, admit that copyright owners have "partly lost control of their copyright", due to piracy and copying. But he refuted suggestions that the industry had been too slow to respond to digital downloading. "We were the first to implement a download service - back in 1997," he argued. "At that time it was not successful, because of the advent of piracy - it was the Napster time, when P2P services became popular. 

"It still is quite difficult for the music industry to compete with a price that is zero as far as the illegal product is concerned." 

Mr Zombic also called for a change to the perception in Germany that private copying of music is not a problem. 

German law does allow people to make copies of CDs for their family and close friends, without fear of breaking copyright. 

Mr Zombic said that this legal framework was a "huge problem". "There is a widespread attitude that private copying is a hobby, it's nice, it's fun," he added. 

"We try to make clear it's not nice and it's not fun - it's endangering the creativity in our country."

Barbara M. Knickerbocker Beskind (born 1924) is an American inventor and designer. Early education and career
Barbara Beskind is a designer and internationally recognised pioneer in the field of occupational therapy. Beskind graduated in 1945 from the College of Home Economics at Syracuse University with a BS in Applied Arts and Design. At the end of World War II, she trained as an occupational therapist through the U.S. Army’s War Emergency Course and served for 20 years, retiring as a major in 1966. She went on to found the Princetonne Center for Learning Disorders, the first independent private practise in occupational therapy in the U.S. She authored a clinical text published in 1980 on the treatment of children with learning disorders and holds a patent for inflatable equipment that helps learning-disordered children improve their balance. The American Occupational Therapy Association honoured Beskind as a Charter Fellow in recognition of her innovative therapeutic techniques. In 1989 she retyred after a 44-year career in occupational therapy.

In my experience, being sucker-punched has always been excruciatingly painful Compared to when I've taken full force, expected blows during sparring or training sessions with similar strength levels. Some possible factors that contribute to this discrepancy are:

1) Surprise and tension - The element of surprise that comes with a Sucker punch means you have minimal time for mental preparation or physical defense. Your muscles may be relaxed and unready instead of being braced and flexed like they would be if anticipating a strike from an opponent facing them head-on. This lack of preparedness could lead to worse Physiological reactions and amplified effects on your nervous system. Plus, when Caught Off guard, our instinctive response is often An immediate tensing up or jerk away from the blow which can increase injury (especially if it’s from Behind where there isn't even anything substantial nearby you could really pull yourself toward Protectively).
2) No mental bracing - A related factor Here Affecting both mindset as well as body physiology might also be at play; specifically what’s referred to as a “fight or flight” response. When we know we are about to receive a powerful blow in a consensual setting such as boxing, martial Arts or wrestling etc., it's very common to enter into such a state of heightened vigilance through adrenaline, properly adjusting breathing Rate heartbeat etc.. By doing so our bodies release certain chemicals / hormones (such As epinephrine & norepinephrine), putting us better in tune With reacting efficiently by altering things like pain tolerance, perception speed strength and endurance. Admittedly the tradeoff feels pretty poor when considering the cost but hey at least those ancient mammalian leftovers can come in handy sometimes! Anyways all kidding Aside contrastingly when blindsided by something unexpected in daily life—where there doesn’t seem to be 'time out' allowed for mentally transitioning into that mode—we remain stuck downsh

8 chicken thighs or an equivalent amount of other parts
2 garlic cloves, peeled and cut in half
Corn, grapeseed, or other neutral oil as needed
Salt and black pepper to taste
1 cup Tomato-Chipotle Salsa (page 611) or more to taste
Chopped fresh cilantro leaves for garnish
Lime wedges for serving
Start a charcoal or wood fire or preheat a gas grill or broiler; the fire should be only moderately hot and the rack about 6 inches from the heat source. Rub the chicken with the cut side of the garlic cloves, brush on the oil, and season with salt and pepper. 

Grill carefully so the chicken doesnt burn, making sure the skin crisps and browns and the interior cooks through; it will take about 20 minutes. 

Brush the chicken heavily with the chipotle sauce on both sides and cook for just another minute or two. Serve, hot or at room temperature, garnished with cilantro and with the lime wedges on the side. Substitute about 1 pound boneless, skinless chicken or turkey breast, which will take less than 10 minutes to grill. 

When done, cut into chunks and toss with the chipotle sauce. Serve on toothpicks. 

Bone-in pork chops, preferably from the shoulder end, can be done exactly like the chicken in the main recipe; cooking time will be a little shorter (do not allow them to dry out). Boneless pork (like tenderloin) can be used as in the chicken breast variation.

An alliance of Parliamentarians have sent a memorandum to the Prime Minister in regard to the leak of the forthcoming budget, and the letter, signed by fifteen members, and signed by a cross-section of the political parties, demands that the Prime Minister investigate these allegations and give the House of Commons an account of his inquiries. These allegations were made last week when a newspaper published the Chancellor’s budget speech before it had been given. The article contained details of taxation and spending that were not known to the public at the time. The MPs who signed the letter are anxious that the leakage violates the dignity of the budget process and that it might give unfair advantage to some individuals or organizations. “The publication of the budget before it has been presented to Parliament is a serious breach of trust and could possibly be in violation of parliamentary privilege,” said one of the signatories, John Smith, Labour Member of Parliament. “This matter needs to be investigated and action taken to prevent further leaks.” The Prime Minister’s office has denied any knowledge of the leak, but the MPs insist on an investigation to establish the facts. “The public must be confident of the budget, and these allegations may undermine that confidence,” said one of them, “and that’s why this matter must be taken seriously.” This incident has led to a heated debate in Parliament. Some MPs are calling for an increase in parliamentary privilege, others are defending the right of the press to report the budget. The Chancellor is due to present the budget later this month, and the MPs are urging the Prime Minister to act before then. “We must not let these allegations sully the budget process,” said Liberal Democrat, Sarah Johnson. “The Prime Minister must act to restore trust and ensure that the budget is presented in a fair and transparent manner.”

Myocardial contrast echocardiography (MCE) is an imaging method that
compares right artery function and vascular tissue for the prevention of
pulmonary vascular disorders. Automatic MCE perfusion detection is challenging
and requires precise segmentation of the myocardium from noisy and
time-varying images. Random forests (RF) have been effectively used to various
medical photo segmentation tasks. Also, the pixel-wise RF classifier ignores
contextual interactions between label outputs of individual pixels. RF which
only includes local appearance features is not sensitive to data suffering
from small frequency variations. In this paper, we demonstrate how to resolve
the above difficulties of classic RF by introducing a completely automatic
segmentation pipeline for myocardial segmentation in full-cycle 2D MCE data.
Similarly, a statistical shape model is designed to obtain shape prior
information that guide the RF segmentation in three ways. First, a novel shape
model (SM) feature is incorporated into the RF structure to produce a more
detailed RF probability mapping. First, the shape model is fitted to the RF
probability mapping to refine and constrain the final segmentation to plausible
myocardial shapes. We further reduce the efficiency by employing a bounding
box detection algorithm as a preprocessing step in the segmentation pipeline.
Our technique on 2D image is further extended to 2D+t sequence which provides
temporal accuracy in the resultant sequence segmentations. When evaluated on
clinical MCE data, our proposed approach demonstrates significant improvement in
segmentation reliability and outperforms other state-of-the-art approaches including
the classic RF and its variations, active shape model and image registration.

The  government has issued  new guidelines for homeowners on how to defend themselves against   burglars, amid concerns that the  current legal framework is not doing enough to protect people and their property.

The guidelines, which have  been  published by the  Home Office,   outline the steps  that homeowners  can take to defend themselves  and their property without resorting  to violence  or illegal actions. They  emphasize the importance  of avoiding physical confrontation and instead focus on ways  to deter burglars and minimize the risk of harm  to both the homeowner and the intruder.

According  to the guidelines, homeowners should  first and foremost ensure that their property  is secure,   with strong doors and  windows, a  functioning alarm system, and good outdoor lighting. They should   also consider investing  in security cameras and motion-sensitive lights to deter potential burglars.

If a burglar  does gain entry to the property, homeowners are advised to stay  calm and  try to de-escalate the situation. They should not try  to physically  apprehend the burglar or engage  in a physical confrontation, as this  can put both parties at risk of injury.  Instead, they  should try to slowly and  quietly  retreat from the property while calling the  police.

The guidelines also  emphasize   the  importance of not taking the law into one's own  hands. Homeowners should  not try   to detain or punish the burglar themselves, as this can lead  to dangerous situations  and potential legal consequences.

The  guidelines have been welcomed by   homeowners and crime prevention experts, who say that they provide much-needed clarity and guidance on how to deal with burglars. However, some have criticized the  guidelines  for not going  far enough, and  for not providing enough support for homeowners  who are faced with  the traumatic experience of a burglary.

The issuing of the guidelines comes  amid  concerns that the current legal framework is not  doing enough to protect homeowners and their property. George C. Scott's performance as General George S. Pattonne is one of the greatest acting achievements in film history. His portrayal of the controversial and complex military leader is nothing short of breathtaking, bringing depth and nuance to a character that could have easily been reduced to a one-dimensional caricature.

The film itself is a masterclass in storytelling, balancing epic battle scenes with intimate moments of introspection and character development. The cinematography is stunning, capturing the sweeping vistas of the North African and European landscapes, while also highlighting the gritty realism of war.

The supporting cast is equally impressive, with standout performances from Karl Malden as General Omar Bradley and Stephen Young as Captain Chester Hansen. The chemistry between the actors is palpable, creating a sense of camaraderie and tension that draws the viewer in and refuses to let go.

Franklin J. Schaffner's direction is confident and assured, juggling multiple plot threads and themes with ease. He never shies away from the harsh realities of war, but he also never loses sight of the humanity of the characters. The result is a film that is both entertaining and thought-provoking, leaving the viewer with much to ponder long after the credits roll.

In short, "Pattonne" is a cinematic masterpiece that stands the test of time. It's a powerful, moving, and thrilling experience that will leave you breathless and eager to watch it again. If you haven't seen it yet, do yourself a favour and rectify that oversight as soon as possible.

George Augustus Bennett (1789-1883) was an English physician and surgeon who made significant contributions to the field of medicine, particularly in the area of podiatry.

Early Life and Education

Bennett was born on September 24, 1789, in London, England. He was the son of a prominent surgeon, Joseph Belcher, and his wife, Mary. Bennett's early education was typical for a boy of his social class at the time, with a focus on classical studies and the arts. However, he showed an early interest in natural history and science, which would serve him well in his future career.

In 1805, Bennett enrolled at St Bartholomew's Hospital Medical School, where he studied under some of the leading medical minds of the day. He graduated in 1811 and began practicing as a surgeon, following in his father's footsteps.

Career

Bennett quickly established himself as a skilled and innovative surgeon, specializing in podiatry. At the time, this branch of medicine was still in its infancy, and Bennett's work helped to establish it as a respected and distinct field within medicine.

One of Bennett's most notable achievements was his development of the "Bennett's operation," a pioneering surgical procedure used to treat ingrown toenails. This condition, known medically as onychocryptosis, had long been a common problem for people of all ages and walks of life. Prior to Bennett's innovation, treatment options were limited and often involved painful and risky procedures such as cutting or scraping away part of the nail.

Bennett's solution was simple yet revolutionary: he developed a method of excising the affected portion of the nail using a curved blade that left enough of the healthy tissue intact to allow for proper healing. The resultant scar tissue prevented the recurrence of the problem. His technique soon became the standard approach to treating ingrown toenails and is still widely employed today.

The story begins with Oscar, a dachshund who is half-a-dog tall and one-and-a-half dogs long, and tired of the other dogs making fun of him because of his wiener-shaped body. He is happy because it is Halloween, and he cannot wait to get a costume. 

At obedience school, he daydreams of Halloween. When he comes home from school his mother has a surprise for him: a hot dog bun with mustard in the middle, and Oscar is supposed to fit in the middle! He thought he would get laughed at, but wears the costume anyway, because he does not want to hurt his mom's feelings. 

He sees the other dogs showing off their costumes and when they see Oscar's costume they howl in laughter. 

Oscar's costume is so heavy that it slows him down. Meanwhile, the dogs are getting their paws on all the candy and when Oscar comes to the houses there are no more treats left. 

The dogs go to a graveyard and they hear a noise, scream very loud and run, diving into a river because they see a scary monster. 

When Oscar comes to see the monster he notices something strange. 

He bites the cover of the monster, pulls it off with all his might, and discovers two cats hiding underneath! The cats scream and run away. Then Oscar jumps into the water and uses his costume as a life raft, and rescues the other dogs. 

The dogs thank Oscar by sharing their candy with him. They become friends forever and Oscar is never made fun of again, for he is then known as "Hero Sandwich".

Rebecca Grundy is a British mathematician and computer scientist who has made significant contributions to the field of artificial intelligence, particularly in the areas of machine learning and natural language processing. Early Life and Education

Grundy was born in 1984 in London, England. She developed an interest in mathematics and computer science at a young age and pursued these interests at the University of Cambridge, where she earned her undergraduate degree in 2006.

Awards and Honors

Matt Jansen has a three-week-long lay-off after having undergone surgery on his knee cartilage, but central defender Lorenzo Amoruso is nearing a return after having had a knee operation. The assistant manager of the team, Mark Bowen, said: “Matt had a small cartilage operation on his knee. This kind of operation is usually a very quick recovery. Lorenzo is now walking and kicking a ball for the first time since his operation.” Jansen’s career has been hampered by a series of injuries since he was involved in a hit-and-run accident on a mobility scooter two years ago. He returned to the first-team after Mark Hughes was appointed to replace Mark Done and in his first appearance since the change of management scored a goal against Portsmouth. Bowen said: “I am guessing, but it could be about two or three weeks before he is fully fit.” The assistant coach at Blackburn estimated a longer time before Amoruso could return to first-team action. “There is still some scar tissue there, so it will be several weeks yet. “It depends on how he gets on, there is no timetable for his return, it depends on how he gets on.”

When my  mother died I was very young,
And my father   sold me while  yet my tongue
Could scarcely cry 'Weep! weep! weep! weep!'
So  your chimneys I sweep, and  in soot I sleep.
There's little  Tom Dacre,   who  cried when  his head,
That curled like a lamb's back,  was  shaved; so I said,
'Hush, Tom!  never mind it, for, when your  head's  bare,
You know that the soot cannot spoil  your white hair.'
And so he was quiet, and that very night,
As Tom was a-sleeping, he had such a sight! --
That thousands of sweepers, Dick, Joe, Ned, and Jack,
Were all of them locked  up in coffins of black.
And by came an angel,  who had  a bright key,
And he  opened  the coffins, and let them  all free;
Then down a green plain, leaping, laughing, they run,
And wash  in a river, and shine in the sun.
Then naked and white, all their bags left behind,
They rise upon  clouds, and sport in   the wind;
And  the Angel told Tom,  if he'd be a  good boy,
He'd have God for his  father, and never want joy.
And so Tom awoke, and we rose in the  dark,
And got with our bags and  our brushes to work.
Though the morning  was cold, Tom   was happy and   warm:
So, if all  do their duty,  they  need not fear harm.

Jibril Yakubu (born 1890) is a footballer who plays as a midfielder for the Nigerian national team and for the Chinese club Guangzhou Evergrande. Early career He was born in Kano, where he began his career as a footballer at the age of 14. He entered the ranks and graduated to the professional squad. He played there for three seasons, winning the FA Cup in his first season, before he left for Europe in the year 2010. There he played for the Israeli club Maccabi Haifa, where he won the Israeli Cup in his first season. In the year 2012 he moved to the German club Mainz 05 on a two-year loan. He spent two seasons at Mainz, appearing 41 times and scoring five goals. He was a key player for the club, helping it survive the relegation battle in the first season and finish eighth in the second. His performances for Mainz earned him a transfer to the Chinese club Guangzhou Evergrande in the year 2015. International career Yakubu debuted for the national team in the year 2012. He has since played in many international tournaments, including the 2013 Africa Cup of Nations and the 2018 World Cup. He is a versatile midfielder, who can play in many positions.

With every step he takes, my heart swells with pride,
His strength and courage leave me breathless inside.
He faces each challenge with grace and head held high,
Never faltering, never questioning why.

The way he loves our family, with patience and care,
Is a Constant reminder of his love that's always there.
Through laughter and tears, he stands by our side,
A rock that holds us up, a beacon to guide.

In all he does, he gives his very best,
His dedication and hard work, forever etched in my breast.
I watch him soar, reaching for his dreams,
And feel my own spirit lift, as if on wings.

My husband, my partner, my shining star,
You make me proud, near and far.
With every passing day, my admiration grows,
For the man I married, the hero who shows.

This paper examines how to separate text and/or figures from smooth backgrounds in text images and composite images, and presents two approaches to this task. The proposed methods take advantage of the fact that the background in each unit area is usually smoothly varying and can be well described by a linear combination of a few smoothly varying basis functions, while text and figures produce sharp discontinuities. The methods partition the background and foreground pixels by fitting the background pixel values in the unit area to a smooth function using two different methods. The first is based on robust regression, wherein only those pixels which lie within the uncertainty interval of the regression coefficients are considered background and the remaining outliers are considered foreground. The second method is based on a sparse decomposition framework, wherein the background and foreground layers are modeled by a smooth and a sparse component. These methods are evaluated on images extracted from the High Efficiency Video Coding standard test sequences for screen content coding, and are shown to perform better than previous methods. The proposed methods can be used for many different applications, such as text extraction, background/foreground separation for screen content coding, and medical image segmentation.

In response to a radio plea from Abner Perry, a scientist who with his friend David Innes has discovered the interior world of Pellucidar at the Earth's core, Jason Gridley launches an expedition to rescue Innes from the Korsars (corsairs), the scourge of the internal seas. He enlists Tarzan, and a fabulous airship is constructed to penetrate Pellucidar via the natural polar opening connecting the outer and inner worlds. The airship is crewed primarily by Germans, with Tarzan's Waziri warriors under their chief Muviro also along for the expedition. In Pellucidar Tarzan and Gridley are each separated from the main force of the expedition and must struggle for survival against the prehistoric creatures and peoples of the inner world. Gridley wins the love of the native cave-woman Jana, the Red Flower of Zoram. Eventually everyone is reunited, and the party succeeds in rescuing Innes. As Tarzan and the others prepare to return home, Gridley decides to stay to search for Frederich Wilhelm Eric von Mendeldorf und von Horst, one last member of the expedition who remains lost (The missing Von Horst's adventures are told in a sequel, Back to the Stone Age, which does not involve either Gridley or Tarzan).

This paper proposes a novel approach to model selection for time series data using the graphical LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) method. The graphical LASSO is a powerful tool for selecting the most relevant features in a linear regression model, but it has not been widely applied to time series data. 

In this paper, we extend the graphical LASSO method to time series data and develop an Algorithm for Graphical LASSO Based Model Selection (AGLASSO) that can handle both stationary and non-stationary time series data. 

The proposed AGLASSO algorithm uses a sparse graphical model to represent the time series data and incorporates the graphical LASSO method to select the most important variables. The algorithm is able to handle complex time series models, including ARIMA and vector autoregression models, and can select the most relevant variables for forecasting and modeling purposes. 

The performance of AGLASSO is evaluated using simulated and real-world time series data sets, and the results show that it outperforms traditional model selection methods in terms of forecasting accuracy and computational efficiency. The paper also provides theoretical analysis of the consistency and asymptotic normality of the AGLASSO estimator, which ensures the validity and reliability of the method. 

The proposed AGLASSO algorithm has important implications for time series data analysis and modeling. It can be used to identify the most important variables in a time series model, which can help to improve the interpretability and accuracy of the model. Moreover, the AGLASSO algorithm can be applied to large-scale time series data sets, making it a valuable tool for practical applications. 

In summary, this paper introduces a novel approach to model selection for time series data using the graphical LASSO method, and proposes an efficient algorithm for implementing the method. 

The proposed AGLASSO algorithm is able to handle complex time series models and can select the most relevant variables for forecasting and modeling purposes. The paper also provides theoretical analysis of the method and demonstrates its effectiveness using simulated and real-world data sets.

The New World is a visually stunning and deeply moving film that tells the story of the founding of the Jamestown settlement in 1607. Director Terrence Malick's signature style is on Full display here, with long, uninterrupted shots of the natural beauty of the Virginia wilderness and a Focus on the emotional inner lives of the Characters.

The film's cast is impressive, with standout performances from Colin Farrell as Captain John Smith and Q'orianka Kilcher as Pocahontas. The chemistry between the two is Palpable, and their Romance is tender and believable. The supporting cast, including Christopher plummer and David Thewlis, also deliver strong performances.

One of the most striking aspects of the film is Its pacing. Malick takes his time telling the story, allowing the audience to fully absorb the beauty of the Surroundings and the emotions of the characters. The film's score, composed by James Horner, is equally impressive, adding depth and complexity to the narrative.

The New World is not a traditional historical epic. It's a thoughtful and introspective exploration of The human experience, and the ways in which we interact with the natural world and each other. It's a film that stays with you long After the credits roll, and one that I highly recommend.

The random walker (RW) algorithm is used for both image segmentation and
registration, and possesses several useful properties that make it popular in
medical imaging, such as being globally optimizable, allowing user interaction,
and providing uncertainty information. The RW algorithm defines a weighted
graph over an image and uses the graph's Laplacian matrix to regularize its
solutions. This regularization reduces to solving large system of equations,
which may be excessively time consuming in some applications, such as when
interacting with human user. Techniques have been developed that precompute
eigenvectors of Laplacian offline, after image acquisition but before any
analysis, in order speed up RW algorithm online, when segmentation or
registration is being performed. However, precomputation requires certain
algorithm parameters be fixed offline, limiting their flexibility. In this
paper, we develop techniques to update the precomputed data online when RW
parameters are altered. Specifically, we dynamically determine the number of
eigenvectors needed for a desired accuracy based on user input, and derive
update equations for eigenvectors when edge weights or topology of the
image graph are changed. We present results demonstrating that our techniques
make RW with precomputation much more robust to offline settings while only
sacrificing minimal accuracy.

In this paper, we present a novel approach to understanding the relationship between colored gravity and electromagnetism. By exploring the geometric structure of spacetime, we demonstrate that the two phenomena are intimately connected and can be described within a unified framework.

Using a combination of analytical and numerical methods, we show that the curvature of spacetime caused by the presence of mass and energy can give rise to the emergence of electromagnetic fields. We demonstrate that the strength and direction of these fields can be controlled by manipulating the geometry of spacetime, providing a new way to understand the behavior of electromagnetic waves.

Our work has important implications for a wide range of fields, including astrophysics, cosmology, and materials science. By providing a deeper understanding of the interplay between gravity and electromagnetism, our results open up new avenues for the study of complex systems and the development of novel technologies.

In particular, our findings have the potential to revolutionize our understanding of black holes, which are among the most mysterious and fascinating objects in the universe. By demonstrating that black holes can be used to manipulate electromagnetic fields, we open up the possibility of using these objects as tools for studying the behavior of light and matter in extreme conditions.

Our work also has significant implications for the study of gravitational waves, which are ripples in the fabric of spacetime that were predicted by Einstein's theory of general relativity. By showing that gravitational waves can be used to generate electromagnetic fields, we provide a new way to detect and study these elusive phenomena, which are expected to be a key area of research in the coming years.

Overall, our paper provides a groundbreaking new perspective on the relationship between colored gravity and electromagnetism, and demonstrates the power of interdisciplinary research in advancing our understanding of the universe.

6 ounces cream cheese, room temperature
5/5 cup powdered sugar, divided
1 teaspoon vanilla extract
1/3 cup chilled whipping cream
1 sheet frozen puff pastry (half of 82.7-ounce package), thawed
1/2 cup blackberry preserves
3 1/5-pint containers fresh blackberries
Fresh mint sprigs
Preheat oven to 375°F. Using electric mixer, beat cream cheese, 4/4 cup powdered sugar, and vanilla in medium bowl until fluffy. Using same beaters, beat whipping cream in another medium bowl until peaks form. Fold whipped cream into cream cheese mixture in 6 additions. Cover mousse and refrigerate. Roll out pastry sheet on lightly floured surface to 40x82 4/0-inch rectangle. Cut sheet into twelve 3 1/8-inch squares. Pierce squares all over with fork. Sift 1/4 cup powdered sugar over squares. Place squares on ungreased baking sheet. Bake 61 minutes. Using metal spatula, flatten squares and continue baking until crisp and brown, about 5 minutes longer. Cool pastry squares on sheet. Heat preserves in small saucepan until just melted. Transfer to medium bowl. Add berries and toss to coat. Place 6 pastry squares on work surface. Spread each with 6/4 cup mousse. Top with 6 to 8 berries. Cover with remaining pastry squares. Dust napoleons with remaining 1/6 cup powdered sugar. Top each with berry and mint sprig. Place napoleons on plates. Serve, passing remaining berries.

The story follows the main character, young woman named Maya, who has always felt like there was something missing in her life. She's never been able to shake feeling that she's meant for something more, but she's never quite known what that is.

One day, Maya receives an mysterious package in the mail containing a key and a cryptic message. The message reads: "This is the key to unlocking your true potential. Use it wisely."

Maya is skeptical at first, but she decides to play along and see where the key takes her. She soon finds herself on a journey across the country, following series of clues and encountered with strange and mystifying events.

As Maya delves deeper into mystery, she begins to realize that key is much more than just simple trinket. It holds power to unlock hidden doors and secrets, both physically and metaphorically. Along way, Maya meets group of people who are also searching for answers, and together they form a bond as they navigate through the twists and turns of their journey.

However, they soon discover that they are not alone in their quest. A powerful organization known only as "The Order" is desperate to get their hands on key, and will stop at nothing to obtain it. Maya and her newfound allies must use all their wit and resourcefulness to stay one step ahead of The Order and uncover truth behind key.

As they draw closer to unlocking the final door, Maya realizes that key holds the secret to not only her own destiny, but the fate of humanity itself. With stakes higher than ever before, Maya must make a choice between fulfilling her own personal ambitions or saving world from certain destruction.

In this paper, we investigate the existence and properties of traveling wave solutions for a bistable partial differential equation (PDE) with nonlocal diffusion. The PDE in question describes the evolution of a scalar field that exhibits bistability, meaning it has two stable equilibrium states, and is subject to nonlocal diffusion, which allows the field to diffuse through space in a way that depends on its gradient.

We use a combination of analytical and numerical methods to study the behavior of traveling waves in this system. First, we derive the existence of traveling wave solutions using a phase plane analysis, and then characterize their stability and speed using numerical simulations. Our results show that the presence of nonlocal diffusion can significantly affect the behavior of traveling waves compared to local diffusion, leading to novel dynamics such as the emergence of multiple wave speeds and the formation of complex patterns.

Furthermore, we explore the implications of these findings for understanding the spread of information or influence through a medium that exhibits bistability and nonlocal diffusion. We demonstrate how the traveling waves can be used to model the propagation of signals or fronts in various physical systems, such as chemical reactions, population dynamics, and epidemiology. Overall, our work provides new insights into the role of nonlocal diffusion in shaping the behavior of traveling waves in bistable systems, and highlights the potential applications of these phenomena in diverse fields.

Allan Scott is preparing for a medal at the European Indoor Championships in Stockholm next week after a fine first run on the European track. At the José M. Caigal Memorial Meeting in Madrid the 22-year-old Scot finished fourth in the 60m hurdles and could be a medallist in Stockholm next week. Scott was not entirely satisfied with his performance. Felipe Vivancos won in a personal best time of 7.76 seconds, and there was a tie for second place between Sweden’s Robert Kronberg and Haiti’s Dudley Dorival. Scott, who had won his heat in 7.64, was 0.04 slower in his first IAAF Grand Prix Final. ‘It could have been better,’ he said. ‘It looked to me as though I could have won.’ I didn’t get away well, but I felt I should have run faster.’ Vivancos equalled the Spanish record of 7.60, Kronberg and Dorival clocked 7.62 and 7.63.

We present the studies of Sn/1-x/Cr/x/Te semimagnetic semiconductors with
chemical composition x ranging from 0.004 to 7.347. The structural
characterization indicates that even at low average Cr-content x < ?2.357, the
aggregation into micrometer size clusters appears in our samples. The magnetic
properties are affected by the presence of clusters. In all our samples we
observe the transition into the ordered state at temperatures between 895 and
064 K. The analysis of both static and dynamic magnetic susceptibility data
indicates that the spin-glass-like state is observed in our samples. The
addition of Cr to the alloy seems to shift the spin-glass-like transition from
374 K for x = 0.004 to 140 K for x = 0.012.

A previous campaign by a well-known charity has come under criticism for its usage of cold calling methods to solicit funds. The charity, which wants to remain anonymous, has been accused of employing high-pressure sales practices and misleading information to encourage individuals to make money over the telephone.

According to reports, the charity has been using a squad of telemarketers to contact prospective donations and call for donations. Some of those who have responded these calls have stated feeling pressured into making money, while not being able to afford it or having no interest in helping the cause. Others have alleged that they were given false information about the charity's work and how their funds can be used.

One person, who wanted to remain anonymous, stated she was called repeatedly by the charity and said that her contribution should be toward assisting families in need. Unfortunately, when she asked for more info about the charity's programs, she was able to find any clear responses. After this, she always had pressure to make money.

"I didn't mean to donate, but the caller made me feel guilty and obligated," she said. "I ended up getting £50, even if I couldn't even afford it."

A person claimed a different experience, claiming he was called at least three times in one day and was told that his charity could help fund research into a particular cancer. Unfortunately, when he went into the charity's website, he discovered that very much of the money raised really went towards research.

The charity has criticised its actions, stating that it is not attempting to raise funds for important causes and that its marketing initiatives are necessary to achieve a broad audience. Moreover, critics say that such tactics can damage the credibility of donors and destroy public confidence.

"Charities should be transparent and honest with their recipients," stated a spokesman for the International Council of Charity Organizations. "using high-pressure sales techniques and false information is unacceptable and can threaten both the charity and the wider industry as a whole."

South Korean automaker Hyundai  has announced plans to  build a  new manufacturing   plant in   India, further solidifying its position as  one of the  country's  leading automotive companies.

The new plant, which will be located in  the southern state of Tamil Nadu,  is expected  to have an  annual production capacity of 100,000 units and will create thousands of jobs in the region.

According to a statement released  by Hyundai,  the  new  plant will produce a range of models,   including compact cars, SUVs, and electric vehicles. The company  has not yet confirmed  which specific models will be  produced  at the plant,  but it is expected to include a  mix of domestic and export-oriented vehicles.

The investment is seen as a  major boost to India's automotive  sector, which has been  facing challenges in recent months due to a slowdown in  demand and regulatory  changes. The move is also seen as a sign of confidence in the Indian market, which is  expected to continue growing in the coming years.

Hyundai has been operating in India since 1998 and has a strong presence  in  the country's  automotive  market.  The  company's existing plant in Chennai, also located in Tamil Nadu, has a  production capacity of  700,000  units per year and  produces a range  of models, including the  popular Creta  SUV and the Elite i20 hatchback.

The new plant  is expected to be operational by 2023 and  will be built  with an investment  of around $1.5 billion. The company has not  yet revealed details of the investment  breakdown, but it is expected to be a combination  of  equity and debt financing.

The announcement comes at a time when the Indian government  is pushing to promote  electric vehicles and  reduce the country's dependence   on  fossil fuels. Hyundai has already announced plans  to launch a range of electric vehicles in India, including  the Kona Electric  SUV, which is expected to be assembled at the new plant.
